## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — HC-SR04 Time-of-Flight Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as Microcontroller
    participant TRIG as HC-SR04<br/>TRIG Pin
    participant TX as Ultrasonic<br/>Transmitter 40 kHz
    participant OBJ as Object<br/>(Reflector)
    participant RX as Ultrasonic<br/>Receiver
    participant ECHO as HC-SR04<br/>ECHO Pin

    Note over MCU,ECHO: Initial State: TRIG=LOW, ECHO=LOW

    MCU->>TRIG: Send 10 microsecond HIGH pulse
    activate TRIG
    TRIG->>TX: Internal circuit triggers
    deactivate TRIG

    TX->>OBJ: Emit 8 cycles of 40 kHz ultrasound burst
    Note over TX,OBJ: Wave travels distance d at speed v
    OBJ-->>RX: Echo reflected back to receiver
    RX->>ECHO: ECHO pin goes HIGH
    activate ECHO
    Note over ECHO: ECHO pin stays HIGH<br/>for time t = 2d / v

    MCU->>ECHO: Measure pulse width t
    ECHO-->>MCU: ECHO returns to LOW
    deactivate ECHO

    Note over MCU: Compute distance:<br/>d = v x t / 2

    MCU->>MCU: d = 343 x t / 2<br/>Convert to cm: d_cm = t_us / 58
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The MCU initiates the sequence with a 10 µs trigger pulse. The sensor emits 8 ultrasound cycles, which travel to the object and back. The ECHO pin's HIGH duration **t** is measured by the MCU. Distance is computed as **d = vt / 2**.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Part A — Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between the sensing principles used in DHT11 and MQ-2 sensors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | DHT11 | MQ-2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Principle** | Capacitive change of polymer dielectric (humidity) + NTC thermistor (temperature) | Chemisorption of gas molecules on tin-dioxide (SnO₂) surface |
| **Output Type** | Digital (1-wire, 40-bit frame) | Analog voltage + Digital (TTL) |
| **Heater Required** | No | Yes (5 V, ~200 mW) |
| **Measurand** | Temperature & Humidity | LPG, smoke, H₂, methane |

> **Awarding Marks:** 1 mark for stating DHT11 uses capacitive + NTC, 1 mark for stating MQ-2 uses SnO₂ chemisorption, 1 mark for one more distinction (output, heater, or measurand).

---

### **Part A — Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: The HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor measures an ECHO pulse width of 4700 µs. Calculate the distance to the object. Assume the speed of sound is 340 m/s.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given:
- Echo pulse width $t = 4700 \text{ µs} = 4.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$
- Speed of sound $v = 340 \text{ m/s}$

Using the time-of-flight equation:

$$d = \frac{v \times t}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{340 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{1.598}{2}$$

$$d = 0.799 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 0.8 \text{ m or 80 \text{ cm}}}$$

> **Awarding Marks:** 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for correct substitution, 1 mark for the final answer with units.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme:** Module 2 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** with an **internal choice**. You attempt **one** of the two options. Each option has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, typically carrying 7 marks each.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

#### **(a) Explain the working principle of the DHT11/DHT22 temperature and humidity sensor with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The DHT11 (and its higher-precision variant DHT22) is a digital-output relative humidity and temperature sensor widely used in IoT applications. It integrates two distinct sensing elements and an 8-bit on-chip microcontroller.

**2. Humidity Sensing — Capacitive Polymer (3 marks):**
The humidity sensing element consists of a thin layer of **hydrophilic polymer** sandwiched between two metallic electrodes, forming a parallel-plate capacitor. The capacitance of this element is given by:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}$$

The polymer's **dielectric constant εᵣ** changes proportionally with the relative humidity of the surrounding air. As water vapour molecules are absorbed, εᵣ rises (water has εᵣ ≈ 80, much higher than the dry polymer), and the capacitance increases linearly with RH.

This capacitor is part of an **RC oscillator circuit** inside the sensor. The oscillation frequency is:

$$f = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$

As *C* rises with humidity, *f* falls. The on-chip MCU counts the oscillator period and converts it to a digital RH value using a factory-calibrated lookup table.

**3. Temperature Sensing — NTC Thermistor (2 marks):**
The temperature element is a **Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor** — a semiconductor whose resistance **decreases exponentially** with temperature, following the B-parameter equation:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$$

The thermistor forms one arm of a **voltage divider**, producing a temperature-dependent voltage:

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{cc}} \times \frac{R_T}{R + R_T}$$

This voltage is digitised by the on-chip ADC, and the MCU applies the inverse B-equation to compute the temperature in °C.

**4. Output — Single-Wire Digital (1 mark):**
The MCU combines both readings into a **40-bit single-wire digital frame** consisting of: 8-bit integer RH + 8-bit decimal RH + 8-bit integer T + 8-bit decimal T + 8-bit checksum. The external MCU reads this frame through a single data line with a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor.

> **Block Diagram (drawn by student):**  
> Humidity (Capacitor) → Oscillator → ADC → MCU → 1-Wire Output  
> Temperature (NTC) → Voltage Divider → ADC → MCU → 1-Wire Output

---

#### **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the operating principle of the MQ-2 gas sensor. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The **MQ-2** is a metal-oxide-semiconductor (MOS) gas sensor used to detect combustible gases such as LPG, propane, methane, hydrogen, and smoke. It belongs to the n-type semiconductor family.

**2. Physical Construction (2 marks):**
The sensor consists of:
- A **ceramic micro-tube** (Al₂O₃) coated with a thin layer of **tin dioxide (SnO₂)** — the active sensing material.
- **Two gold electrodes** on the SnO₂ surface, connected to pins **A and B** (signal output).
- A **nichrome heater coil** inside the tube, connected to pins **H1 and H2**, which maintains the SnO₂ at ~300 °C.
- A **stainless-steel mesh** anti-explosion housing.

**3. Operating Principle in Clean Air (2 marks):**
At ~300 °C, atmospheric **oxygen molecules** adsorb onto the SnO₂ surface and **trap free electrons** from the conduction band, forming negative oxygen ions:

$$O_2 + e^- \rightarrow O_2^{-}_{\text{(ads)}}$$

This creates a **depletion layer** (electron-poor region) around each SnO₂ grain, raising the **inter-grain potential barrier**. The sensor's resistance is therefore **high** (typically 10–100 kΩ).

**4. Operating Principle in Presence of Target Gas (2 marks):**
When a combustible gas (e.g., LPG) is present, it reacts with the adsorbed oxygen ions, releasing the trapped electrons back into the conduction band:

$$\text{LPG} + O_2^{-}_{\text{ads}} \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + e^-$$

The depletion layer shrinks, the potential barrier **drops**, and the SnO₂ grains conduct more easily. The sensor's resistance **Rₛ falls** (often to 1–10 kΩ for high gas concentration). A **load resistor R_L** in series with the sensor forms a voltage divider, producing an analog voltage:

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{cc}} \times \frac{R_L}{R_S + R_L}$$

The output voltage is read by the MCU's ADC. The gas concentration in ppm is computed from the sensitivity ratio **Rₛ/R₀** using the calibration curve.

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### **(a) Explain the operating principle of the HC-SR04 ultrasonic distance sensor with a neat timing diagram. How is the distance computed from the ECHO pulse width? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The **HC-SR04** is a non-contact ultrasonic distance sensor that measures distance by transmitting a 40 kHz ultrasonic burst and timing the echo. It has two identical piezoelectric transducers — one transmitter (TX) and one receiver (RX).

**2. Transmitter Section (1 mark):**
The MCU initiates a measurement by sending a **TRIG pulse** of at least **10 µs** to the TRIG pin. The sensor's internal circuit drives the **TX piezoelectric transducer** to emit **8 cycles of 40 kHz** ultrasound. The choice of 40 kHz is deliberate — it is well above human hearing (20 kHz), is highly directional (~15° cone), and matches the natural resonance of cheap piezo discs.

**3. Propagation and Reflection (1 mark):**
The ultrasound wave propagates through air at the **speed of sound** ($v \approx 343$ m/s at 20 °C). When it strikes an object, it reflects (echo) and travels back to the sensor.

**4. Receiver Section (2 marks):**
The **RX piezoelectric transducer** detects the returning echo. Internally, the sensor sets the **ECHO pin HIGH** the instant the echo is detected and pulls it LOW after a fixed number of cycles. The duration the ECHO pin stays HIGH equals the **round-trip time** of the ultrasound pulse.

**5. Distance Computation (2 marks):**
The MCU measures the ECHO pulse width **t** using a hardware timer (or the `pulseIn()` function in Arduino). The one-way distance *d* to the object is then:

$$d = \frac{v \times t}{2}$$

- **Speed of sound correction:** For better accuracy, the speed of sound is corrected for temperature: $v = 331.3 + 0.606 \, T$ (m/s, *T* in °C).
- **Conversion to cm:** Since $v \approx 343$ m/s = 0.0343 cm/µs, the formula becomes the popular shortcut: $d \text{ (cm)} = t \text{ (µs)} / 58$ (which is **1 / (0.0343 × 2)**).

> **Timing Diagram (drawn by student):**
> ```
> TRIG:  ___|‾‾‾|__________________________________________
> TX:    ______________|∿∿∿∿∿∿∿∿|_________________________
> ECHO:  ____________________________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|______
>                              <-- t (pulse width) -->
> d = v * t / 2
> ```

---

#### **(b) An HC-SR04 sensor returns an ECHO pulse width of 4500 µs at an ambient temperature of 25 °C. Compute the distance to the object in cm. If the temperature rises to 40 °C, what is the new distance reading (assuming the object has not moved)? Comment on the result. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $t_1 = 4500 \text{ µs} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$ (echo at 25 °C)
- $T_1 = 25 \text{ °C}$ and $T_2 = 40 \text{ °C}$

**Step 1 — Speed of sound at 25 °C (1 mark):**

$$v_1 = 331.3 + 0.606 \times 25 = 331.3 + 15.15 = 346.45 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Distance at 25 °C (1 mark):**

$$d_1 = \frac{v_1 \times t_1}{2} = \frac{346.45 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d_1 = \frac{1.559}{2} = 0.7796 \text{ m} = 77.96 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Speed of sound at 40 °C (1 mark):**

$$v_2 = 331.3 + 0.606 \times 40 = 331.3 + 24.24 = 355.54 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — New distance computed using $v_2$ (1 mark):**

$$d_2 = \frac{v_2 \times t_1}{2} = \frac{355.54 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d_2 = \frac{1.5999}{2} = 0.8000 \text{ m} = 80.00 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Difference and comment (3 marks):**

$$\Delta d = d_2 - d_1 = 80.00 - 77.96 = 2.04 \text{ cm}$$

**Comment:**  
The temperature change of 15 °C caused the **apparent distance** to increase by ~2 cm (~2.6 %) even though the object did not move. This is because the **speed of sound increases with temperature** (warmer air is less dense, sound propagates faster). If the MCU uses a fixed value of *v* = 343 m/s without temperature compensation, the error in distance reading will be:

$$\text{Error at 40 °C} = \frac{|80 - (343 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3} / 2 \times 100)|}{80} \times 100 \approx 3.6 \text{ \%}$$

For **high-precision applications** (e.g., scientific instrumentation, precision robotics), the HC-SR04 must be paired with a **temperature sensor** (e.g., DS18B20) and the speed of sound must be computed dynamically.

> **Conclusion:** Temperature compensation is essential for accurate ultrasonic ranging.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 2 Sensor Questions:**

1. **HC-SR04 — Forgetting the "/2":**  
   The single most common error is writing **d = v × t** instead of **d = v × t / 2**. This loses 1–2 marks immediately.  
   *Examiner's tip:* Always write down the physical meaning of the formula ("round-trip divided by 2 gives one-way distance") next to the equation.

2. **MQ Series — Forgetting the Heater:**  
   Many students describe the SnO₂ surface reaction but forget to mention the **heater coil** and the 300 °C operating temperature. The sensor **cannot function** without the heater — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

3. **DHT11/22 — Confusing Capacitive and Resistive Elements:**  
   Some students mix up which element senses humidity and which senses temperature. The correct pairing: **Humidity = Capacitive**, **Temperature = NTC (Resistive)**.

4. **MQ Series — Giving the wrong gas for a sensor variant:**  
   MQ-2 → LPG/smoke (NOT CO), MQ-7 → CO (NOT LPG), MQ-135 → NH₃/NOx (NOT LPG). Confusing these is a 1-mark deduction.

5. **DHT11/22 — Forgetting the 1-wire digital protocol:**  
   The output is **digital**, not analog. Saying "analog voltage output" loses a mark.

6. **HC-SR04 — Not mentioning the 40 kHz burst:**  
   The "ultrasonic" in the name comes from the **40 kHz** frequency, which is **2× the upper audible limit**. Omitting this loses a mark.

7. **Unit Mistakes:**  
   - Distance in **cm** (not metres) is preferred for HC-SR04 in KTU papers.
   - Gas concentration in **ppm** (not percentage).
   - Temperature in **°C** (not Kelvin) for DHT.

8. **NTC Thermistor Direction:**  
   NTC = resistance **decreases** with temperature (Negative Coefficient). PTC = resistance **increases** with temperature. Mixing these up is a 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 (Sensors)**

### **DHT11 / DHT22**
- ✅ **Humidity sensor = Capacitive polymer** (dielectric constant changes with moisture).
- ✅ **Temperature sensor = NTC thermistor** (resistance decreases exponentially with temperature).
- ✅ **Equation:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ and $R_T = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$.
- ✅ **Output:** 1-wire digital, 40-bit frame, with 10 kΩ pull-up.
- ✅ **DHT11:** cheaper (±2 °C, ±5 % RH), 0–50 °C.
- ✅ **DHT22:** more accurate (±0.5 °C, ±2–5 % RH), −40 to +80 °C.
- ✅ **Sampling rate:** 1 Hz (DHT11), 0.5 Hz (DHT22).

### **MQ Series Gas Sensors**
- ✅ **Active material:** Tin dioxide (SnO₂) on ceramic tube.
- ✅ **Physical effect:** Chemisorption of oxygen → trapping of electrons → potential barrier → HIGH Rₛ in clean air.
- ✅ **In gas presence:** Gas reacts with O⁻, releases electrons, drops barrier → LOW Rₛ.
- ✅ **Heater:** H1-H2 pins, 5 V, ~200 mW, ~300 °C. **Sensor fails without heater.**
- ✅ **Output:** Analog voltage via load resistor + digital TTL via LM393 comparator.
- ✅ **Calibration:** $R_S / R_0 = A \cdot C^{-B}$, with $R_0$ measured at 1000 ppm reference gas.
- ✅ **MQ-2:** LPG/smoke. **MQ-7:** CO. **MQ-135:** NH₃/NOx/air quality.

### **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor**
- ✅ **Frequency:** 40 kHz (twice the upper audible limit).
- ✅ **Transducers:** 2 identical piezo discs — one TX, one RX.
- ✅ **Trigger:** ≥ 10 µs HIGH pulse on TRIG pin.
- ✅ **Burst:** 8 cycles of 40 kHz ultrasound.
- ✅ **Echo:** ECHO pin goes HIGH for the round-trip time.
- ✅ **Distance formula:** $d = v \cdot t / 2$ (the **/2** is critical!).
- ✅ **Speed of sound:** $v = 331.3 + 0.606 \, T$ m/s, or ~343 m/s at 20 °C.
- ✅ **Range:** 2 cm – 400 cm, accuracy ±3 mm.
- ✅ **Shortcut:** $d \text{ (cm)} = t \text{ (µs)} / 58$.

### **Cross-Cutting Concepts**
- ✅ All three sensors are **passive** (no actuation), but MQ requires **active heating**.
- ✅ DHT and HC-SR04 give **digital outputs**; MQ gives **analog + digital**.
- ✅ All three need **3.3 V or 5 V supply** and interface directly with an MCU.
- ✅ None of these sensors are "plug-and-forget" — they need **calibration** (DHT LUT, MQ reference resistance R₀, HC-SR04 temperature compensation).

### **KTU 2024 Weightage Expectation**
- 📌 **Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks):** 1 question on DHT, 1 question on either MQ or HC-SR04.
- 📌 **Part B (1 × 14 = 14 marks):** Choice between (DHT + MQ) and (HC-SR04 + numerical).

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw a **neat labelled block diagram** (sensing element → conditioning → output). It is worth **2–3 marks** by itself, and reduces the length of textual explanation you need to write.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators (Working Principles Only) - DC Motors, Solenoid-Based Valves, Electromechanical Relay, Solid State Relay, and Stepper Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET785 - INTERNET OF THINGS BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Actuators (Working Principles Only) - DC Motors, Solenoid-Based Valves, Electromechanical Relay, Solid State Relay, and Stepper Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Actuator in IoT?

An **Actuator** is a physical output device that converts an electrical control signal (typically low-power, coming from a microcontroller, PLC, or IoT edge node) into a corresponding physical action such as motion, rotation, linear displacement, heat, light, or fluid/gas flow. In the **sense–decide–actuate** paradigm of any IoT system, sensors *sense*, processors *decide*, and actuators *act*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEAET785 - Module 2):**
> Actuators are the "effectors" of an IoT node. While sensors bring the physical world *into* the digital domain, actuators push digital decisions *back* into the physical world. Without actuators, an IoT system would be a passive monitoring device only.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Muscle" of IoT

If the **microcontroller is the brain** of an embedded/IoT system, then the **actuator is its muscle**.

- When you press a button on a smart-home app (digital command) and your curtains slide open (physical motion) → a **DC motor / stepper motor** is doing the work.
- When a soil-moisture sensor detects dry soil and a water valve opens automatically → a **solenoid valve** is the actuator.
- When a smart energy meter needs to cut off a 230 V AC load in response to an overload command from a 3.3 V MCU → an **electromechanical or solid-state relay** is the muscle.

### 1.3 Classification of Actuators Covered in KTU Module 2

| Sl. No. | Actuator | Physical Action Produced | Typical IoT Use Case |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | DC Motor | Continuous rotary motion | Robotic wheels, fans, pumps, drones |
| 2 | Solenoid Valve | Linear (push/pull) motion → fluid control | Smart irrigation, gas shut-off, vending machines |
| 3 | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) | Electrical switching via magnetic pull | Smart plugs, HVAC control, industrial control panels |
| 4 | Solid State Relay (SSR) | Electrical switching via semiconductor | High-speed switching, silent operation, medical/industrial |
| 5 | Stepper Motor | Discrete, precise angular rotation | 3D printers, CNC, camera gimbals, valve positioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only "Working Principles"?**
> The KTU Module 2 syllabus explicitly restricts the scope to **working principles only** — not the detailed mathematical modeling of magnetic circuits or full drive-circuit design. However, key equations (torque, back-EMF, step angle, solenoid force) are still expected for Part B questions.

### 1.4 Common Input/Output Characteristics of All Actuators

Every actuator, regardless of type, exhibits the following block-level signal flow:

1. **Control input:** A small electrical signal (logic level, PWM, current, or voltage).
2. **Energy conversion stage:** Electrical energy is converted to mechanical work (or vice-versa in the case of generators).
3. **Physical output:** Motion, force, displacement, or switching action.
4. **Feedback (optional):** Encoders, Hall sensors, or limit switches report back the actuator's actual state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC Motor — Working Principle

#### 2.1.1 Construction
A DC motor has two main parts:
- **Stator (Field):** Provides a static magnetic field. Can be a permanent magnet (PMDC) or an electromagnet.
- **Rotor (Armature):** A coil of wire wound on a laminated iron core, mounted on a shaft.
- **Commutator & Brushes:** Mechanical switch that reverses the current direction in the armature coil every half rotation, ensuring continuous unidirectional torque.

#### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Lorentz Force)

When current $I$ flows through a conductor of effective length $L$ placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$, a force $F$ is exerted on the conductor, given by the **Lorentz force equation**:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$

This force acts tangentially on the armature conductors, producing a **torque** $T$ on the rotor:

$$T = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

Where:
- $k_t$ = motor torque constant (Nm/A)
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux per pole (Weber, Wb)
- $I_a$ = armature current (A)

#### 2.1.3 Back-EMF
As the armature rotates in the magnetic field, it itself acts like a generator and produces a **back-EMF** ($E_b$), which opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's Law):

$$E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

Where $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s). The applied voltage equation becomes:

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$

#### 2.1.4 Direction & Speed Control
- **Direction:** Reversed by swapping the polarity of either the armature or the field (but not both).
- **Speed:** Controlled by varying the armature voltage (using PWM from an MCU such as Arduino/ESP32) — this is the **dominant IoT control method**.

---

### 2.2 Solenoid-Based Valves — Working Principle

#### 2.2.1 Definition
A **solenoid** is a coil of wire wound in a helical shape. When current flows through it, it generates a magnetic field along its axis, just like a bar magnet.

#### 2.2.2 Construction
- **Coil (solenoid winding):** Copper wire wound around a hollow cylindrical form.
- **Plunger (armature):** A soft ferromagnetic piston that sits partially inside the coil.
- **Spring:** Returns the plunger to its rest position when the coil is de-energized.
- **Valve seat/orifice:** The plunger, when pulled into the coil, either opens or closes a fluid/gas passage.

#### 2.2.3 Working Principle
When the coil is energized:
1. Current produces a strong axial magnetic field.
2. The magnetic flux pulls the ferromagnetic plunger into the center of the coil.
3. Plunger motion opens (or closes) the valve orifice, allowing (or stopping) fluid flow.
4. When de-energized, the spring pushes the plunger back to its default position.

The force produced by a solenoid can be approximated by:

$$F = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of turns in the coil
- $I$ = coil current (A)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the plunger ($m^2$)
- $g$ = air-gap length (m)
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space = $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

#### 2.2.4 Valve Configurations
- **Normally Closed (NC):** Valve is closed when coil is OFF; opens only when energized.
- **Normally Open (NO):** Valve is open when coil is OFF; closes only when energized.
- **Two-way, three-way, four-way:** Number of ports available.

---

### 2.3 Electromechanical Relay (EMR) — Working Principle

#### 2.3.1 Definition
An **electromechanical relay (EMR)** is an electrically operated switch that uses an electromagnet (solenoid) to mechanically pull a set of contacts closed or open, thereby switching a separate (usually higher-power) circuit.

#### 2.3.2 Construction
- **Coil (electromagnet):** Many turns of fine wire wound on an iron core.
- **Armature:** A movable iron lever attracted to the coil when energized.
- **Contacts:**
  - **Common (COM):** The wiper terminal.
  - **Normally Open (NO):** Contact open when coil is de-energized.
  - **Normally Closed (NC):** Contact closed when coil is de-energized.
- **Spring:** Returns armature to default position.
- **Yoke/frame:** Provides the magnetic circuit return path.

#### 2.3.3 Working Principle
1. A small control current is applied to the coil.
2. The energized coil generates a magnetic flux that attracts the soft-iron armature.
3. The armature pivots, closing (or opening) the contact pair.
4. The high-power load circuit is now either energized (NO closed) or de-energized (NC opened).
5. When the coil is de-energized, the spring pulls the armature back.

> [!NOTE]
> **EMR in IoT:** A typical 5 V or 12 V relay coil is driven directly by a transistor/MOSFET switch from an MCU GPIO pin, allowing a 3.3 V logic signal to control a 230 V AC appliance.

---

### 2.4 Solid State Relay (SSR) — Working Principle

#### 2.4.1 Definition
A **Solid State Relay (SSR)** performs the *same function* as an EMR — switching an electrical load — but **without any moving parts**. Switching is achieved using semiconductor devices such as TRIACs, SCRs, MOSFETs, or IGBTs.

#### 2.4.2 Construction (Block Diagram)
An SSR typically contains four isolation/switching stages:
1. **Input circuit (LED):** An infrared LED converts the input control signal to light.
2. **Opto-coupler (isolation):** A phototransistor or phototriac on the receiving side detects the LED's light, providing **galvanic isolation** between low-voltage control and high-voltage load.
3. **Trigger/zero-cross circuit:** Detects the AC mains zero-crossing point to switch the load only at near-zero voltage, reducing EMI.
4. **Output switching element:** TRIAC (for AC loads) or MOSFET/IGBT (for DC loads).

#### 2.4.3 Working Principle
1. A small DC voltage applied to the input LED.
2. The LED emits infrared light, which falls on the photosensitive semiconductor.
3. This generates a gate-trigger signal for the output switching element (TRIAC/MOSFET).
4. The output element conducts, completing the load circuit.
5. Switching is **silent, contactless, and extremely fast** (microseconds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero-Cross Switching in AC SSRs:**
> Most AC-output SSRs use *zero-cross detection* — they only turn ON when the AC mains voltage crosses through 0 V. This drastically reduces inrush current, RFI/EMI, and stress on the load.

---

### 2.5 Stepper Motor — Working Principle

#### 2.5.1 Definition
A **stepper motor** is a brushless DC motor that divides a full rotation ($360^{\circ}$) into a large number of **equal discrete steps**. By sending a specific sequence of pulses to its coils, the shaft can be made to rotate *one step at a time*, with each step being a precise, known angle.

#### 2.5.2 Construction
- **Stator:** Multiple toothed electromagnets arranged in phases (commonly 4 phases).
- **Rotor:** A toothed permanent magnet (or a soft-iron variable reluctance rotor). Rotor teeth align precisely with energized stator teeth.
- **Bearings & shaft:** Provide mechanical support and deliver output rotation.

#### 2.5.3 Working Principle
1. The stator has multiple phase windings. When a particular phase is energized, it magnetically attracts the nearest rotor tooth.
2. By sequentially energizing the phase windings in a specific order, the rotor is made to "step" from one position to the next.
3. The motor continues to step as long as the phase-energizing sequence continues.
4. The **direction of rotation** can be reversed by reversing the energizing sequence.

The **step angle** $\theta_s$ is given by:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_s \cdot m}$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = number of stator teeth (or magnetic poles)
- $m$ = number of phases × gear ratio (for hybrid steppers with reduction)

For a simple **variable reluctance stepper** with $N_r$ rotor teeth and $N_s$ stator teeth:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r}$$

The number of steps per revolution:

$$\text{Steps per revolution} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s}$$

#### 2.5.4 Stepping Modes
- **Full-step (single-coil excitation):** One phase energized at a time.
- **Full-step (dual-coil excitation):** Two adjacent phases energized — higher torque.
- **Half-step:** Alternates single and dual excitation → doubles resolution.
- **Microstepping:** Uses sinusoidal current waveforms → sub-degree resolution.

#### 2.5.5 Open-Loop Position Control
A major advantage in IoT/embedded systems: the position of the shaft is **known by counting pulses sent**, with no encoder feedback required. This makes stepper motors ideal for **camera gimbals, 3D printers, CNC, valve-position control**.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Force on armature conductor | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ | N | Lorentz force |
| 2 | Motor torque | $T = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Nm | $k_t$ in Nm/A |
| 3 | Back-EMF | $E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$ | V | Opposes supply voltage |
| 4 | DC motor voltage equation | $V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$ | V | KVL of armature loop |
| 5 | Solenoid pulling force | $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g^2}$ | N | Inversely $\propto g^2$ |
| 6 | Solenoid inductance | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{\ell}$ | H | $\ell$ = coil length |
| 7 | Step angle (stepper) | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_r}$ | degree | $N_r$ = rotor teeth |
| 8 | Steps per revolution | $\text{SPR} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s}$ | steps | Integer value |
| 9 | Stepper shaft angular speed | $\omega = \theta_s \cdot f_p$ | rad/s | $f_p$ = pulse rate (Hz) |
| 10 | Power in DC motor | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | W | Mechanical output |
| 11 | Pull-in / pull-out torque | $T \propto \dfrac{1}{f_p^2}$ (approx.) | Nm | Torque falls with speed |
| 12 | Relay coil MMF | $\text{MMF} = N \cdot I$ | Ampere-turns | Magnetizing force |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Formulas (1), (3), (4), (7), and (8) are the **most frequently tested** in Module 2 actuator problems. Memorize them along with their units — partial marks depend on correct units.

### 2.7 Why These Actuators Matter in IoT — Real-World Utility

- **DC Motors** → drone propellers, smart fans, robotic vacuum cleaners (e.g., Roomba), water pumps in smart agriculture.
- **Solenoid Valves** → automatic irrigation valves, LPG gas-leak shutoff, smart coffee machines, blood-pressure cuffs.
- **EMR** → legacy industrial control, smart-switch retrofits for AC appliances, automotive horn/light control.
- **SSR** → medical equipment (silent operation), semiconductor manufacturing (cleanroom, no arcing), temperature controllers in ovens.
- **Stepper Motors** → 3D printer extruder/bed drives, camera pan-tilt-zoom (PTZ), telescope tracking, precision valve actuation in chemical plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the core derivations that KTU examiners expect in **Part B (14-mark) questions**.

### 3.1 Derivation of the DC Motor Torque Equation

**Given:** A DC motor armature with $Z$ total conductors, arranged in $A$ parallel paths, each conductor of effective length $L$, carrying current $I_a$, in a magnetic flux per pole $\Phi$.

**Step 1 — Current per conductor:**

Since the armature has $Z$ conductors divided into $A$ parallel paths, the current through each conductor is:

$$I_{cond} = \frac{I_a}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Force on one conductor:**

Applying the Lorentz force equation to a single conductor:

$$F_{cond} = B \cdot I_{cond} \cdot L$$

But the flux density at the air-gap can be written in terms of flux per pole $\Phi$ and pole area $a$:

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{a}$$

Hence:

$$F_{cond} = \frac{\Phi \cdot I_{cond} \cdot L}{a} = \frac{\Phi \cdot I_a \cdot L}{a \cdot A}$$

**Step 3 — Torque on one conductor:**

Each conductor sits at a radius $r$ from the shaft axis. The torque produced by one conductor is:

$$T_{cond} = F_{cond} \cdot r = \frac{\Phi \cdot I_a \cdot L \cdot r}{a \cdot A}$$

**Step 4 — Total armature torque:**

All $Z$ conductors contribute torque. Therefore:

$$T_a = Z \cdot T_{cond} = \frac{Z \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \cdot L \cdot r}{a \cdot A}$$

**Step 5 — Group the geometric constants:**

Define the machine constant $k_a = \dfrac{Z \cdot L \cdot r}{a \cdot A}$ (depends only on machine geometry):

$$T_a = k_a \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

This is conventionally written as:

$$\boxed{T_a = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}$$

where $k_t$ is the motor torque constant.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Back-EMF Equation in DC Motor

**Step 1 — EMF induced in one conductor:**

Each conductor moves through a magnetic flux of $\Phi$ Webbers per pole per revolution. The number of flux cuts per revolution is:

$$\text{Flux cuts per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \cdot P$$

where $P$ is the number of poles.

**Step 2 — Rate of flux cutting:**

For a rotor spinning at $N$ revolutions per second:

$$\text{Flux cuts per conductor per second} = \Phi \cdot P \cdot N$$

**Step 3 — EMF per conductor (Faraday's Law):**

$$e_{cond} = \Phi \cdot P \cdot N$$

**Step 4 — Total back-EMF:**

With $Z$ conductors in $A$ parallel paths, the conductors per path = $Z/A$. The conductors in any path are effectively in series for EMF, so:

$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \Phi \cdot P \cdot N$$

**Step 5 — Group constants:**

Define $k_e = \dfrac{Z \cdot P}{A}$. Since $N = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ (where $\omega$ is in rev/s × $2\pi$):

$$E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot N = k_e' \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

$$\boxed{E_b = k_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In SI units, for a DC motor, $k_e$ (V·s/rad) and $k_t$ (Nm/A) are numerically equal when consistent units are used. This is a consequence of energy conservation (electrical power in = mechanical power out at steady state).

---

### 3.3 Solenoid Force — Full Derivation from Energy Method

**Step 1 — Magnetic energy stored in the air-gap:**

The magnetic energy stored in an air-gap of length $g$, cross-section $A$, and permeability $\mu_0$ is:

$$W_{mag} = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot A \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Flux density in terms of coil MMF:**

By Ampere's law applied around the magnetic path, neglecting iron reluctance:

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I}{g}$$

Substituting:

$$W_{mag} = \frac{1}{2 \mu_0} \cdot \left(\frac{\mu_0 N I}{g}\right)^2 \cdot A \cdot g = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g}$$

**Step 3 — Force as negative gradient of energy:**

At constant current (typical for a solenoid held at a constant voltage), force on the plunger is:

$$F = -\frac{\partial W_{mag}}{\partial g} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g^2}$$

$$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g^2}}$$

**Interpretation:** The force is **inversely proportional to the square of the air-gap** — this is why a solenoid's pull-in force is dramatically larger near its closed position.

---

### 3.4 Step Angle Derivation for a Variable-Reluctance Stepper Motor

**Step 1 — Rotor geometry:**

A variable-reluctance stepper has $N_r$ equally spaced teeth on its rotor. The teeth are uniformly distributed around $360^{\circ}$.

**Step 2 — Angular pitch of rotor teeth:**

The angular pitch between adjacent rotor teeth is:

$$\theta_r = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r}$$

**Step 3 — Stable equilibrium condition:**

A rotor tooth always aligns with a stator pole that is energized (since the air-gap reluctance is minimized there). Switching excitation to the next stator phase shifts the equilibrium by exactly one rotor-tooth pitch.

**Step 4 — Step angle:**

Therefore, each electrical step rotates the rotor by exactly one rotor-tooth pitch:

$$\boxed{\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r}}$$

**Worked Example:** A stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth. Then:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{50} = 7.2^{\circ} \text{ per step}$$

Number of steps per revolution:

$$\text{SPR} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{7.2^{\circ}} = 50 \text{ steps/rev}$$

If the input pulse frequency is $f_p = 500$ Hz, then the shaft speed is:

$$\omega = \theta_s \cdot f_p = 7.2^{\circ} \times 500 = 3600^{\circ}/\text{s} = 10 \text{ rev/s} = 600 \text{ RPM}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EMR vs SSR

| Parameter | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid State Relay (SSR) | Engineering Implication |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Switching element | Moving armature + contacts | Semiconductor (TRIAC/MOSFET) | SSR has no wear |
| Switching speed | $5 - 15$ ms | $0.1 - 1$ ms | SSR is $50\times$ faster |
| Lifetime | $10^5 - 10^6$ operations | $>10^9$ operations | SSR is more durable |
| Acoustic noise | Audible "click" | Silent | SSR preferred in hospitals |
| Contact bounce | Yes (1-2 ms) | None | SSR gives clean switching |
| Isolation | Magnetic (coil → contacts) | Optical (opto-coupler) | Both give isolation |
| On-state voltage drop | $\approx 0$ V (metal contact) | $1 - 2$ V (semiconductor) | EMR more efficient |
| Off-state leakage | None (air gap) | $1 - 10$ mA | SSR cannot fully "open" |
| Surge handling | High (contacts survive inrush) | Low (semiconductors fragile) | EMR better for motor loads |
| Cost per amp | Low | High | EMR economical for low-current |
| Vibration sensitivity | Yes (contacts may bounce) | No | SSR ideal for moving platforms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT Actuator Block Diagram — Sense → Decide → Actuate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical World<br/>Temperature, Humidity, Motion, Gas] --> B[Sensor Node<br/>DHT22, PIR, MQ-2, LDR]
    B --> C[Microcontroller<br/>ESP32 / Arduino / Raspberry Pi]
    C --> D[Actuator Driver<br/>MOSFET / Transistor / H-Bridge / Opto-isolator]
    D --> E[Actuator<br/>DC Motor / Solenoid / EMR / SSR / Stepper]
    E --> F[Physical Action<br/>Spin, Open Valve, Switch Load, Move Shaft]
    F --> G[Effect on Environment<br/>Cooling, Irrigation, Power Cut, Precision Move]
    G -. feedback .-> A

    style A fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style B fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1
    style C fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d
    style D fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d
    style E fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b
    style F fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6
    style G fill:#fef08a,stroke:#854d0e
```

---

### 4.2 DC Motor Internal Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Positive<br/>Terminal] --> B1[Brush 1]
    B1 --> C1[Commutator<br/>Segment 1]
    C1 --> W[Armature<br/>Coil Winding]
    W --> C2[Commutator<br/>Segment 2]
    C2 --> B2[Brush 2]
    B2 --> N[Negative<br/>Terminal]
    S[Shaft] --- W
    M[Permanent<br/>Magnet N] --- W
    M2[Permanent<br/>Magnet S] --- W

    style P fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534
    style N fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#991b1b
    style W fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style M fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style M2 fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#1e3a8a
```

---

### 4.3 Solenoid Valve Cut-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Coil["Solenoid Coil (De-energized State)"]
        C1[Copper Windings N turns]
        S1[Spring - Compressed]
        PL[Plunger - Outside Coil]
        OR1[Valve Orifice - CLOSED]
        FL[Fluid Inlet]
        FL2[Fluid Outlet]
    end

    C1 -->|magnetic field| PL
    S1 -->|pushes back| PL
    PL -->|when energized| MOV[Moves Down Into Coil]
    MOV -->|uncovers| OR2[Valve Orifice - OPEN]
    OR2 --> ALLOW[Allows Fluid Flow]
    FL --> OR2
    OR2 --> FL2

    style C1 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style S1 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
    style PL fill:#d1d5db,stroke:#374151
    style OR1 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#991b1b
    style OR2 fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534
    style FL fill:#a5f3fc,stroke:#155e75
    style FL2 fill:#a5f3fc,stroke:#155e75
```

---

### 4.4 Electromechanical Relay — Internal Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Terminal A1] --> COIL[Solenoid Coil<br/>N turns]
    B[Input Terminal A2] --> COIL
    COIL -->|generates flux| Y[Yoke Core]
    Y --> ARM[Armature<br/>Pivoting Iron Lever]
    ARM -->|spring pulls back| SP[Return Spring]
    ARM -->|when attracted| MOV[Moves Downward]
    MOV --> CONT[Contact Pair]
    CONT --> C[Common COM]
    CONT --> NO[Normally Open NO]
    CONT --> NC[Normally Closed NC]
    NO --> L1[Load Terminal 1]
    NC --> L2[Load Terminal 2]

    style COIL fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style ARM fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style CONT fill:#d1d5db,stroke:#374151
    style NO fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534
    style NC fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#991b1b
```

---

### 4.5 Solid State Relay — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input +] --> LED[Infrared LED]
    IN2[Input -] --> LED
    LED -->|light beam| OPT[Opto-Coupler<br/>Phototriac or Phototransistor]
    OPT -->|isolated signal| ZC[Zero-Cross<br/>Detection Circuit]
    ZC -->|gate trigger| TR[TRIAC for AC<br/>or MOSFET for DC]
    TR --> LOUT[Load Output Terminals]
    LOUT --> LOAD[AC or DC Load]

    style LED fill:#fef08a,stroke:#854d0e
    style OPT fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style ZC fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8
    style TR fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b
    style LOAD fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d
```

---

### 4.6 Stepper Motor — Coil Energization Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MCU["Microcontroller / Driver Board"]
        S1[Step Pulse Generator]
    end
    S1 --> D[Driver IC<br/>ULN2003 / A4988 / DRV8825]
    D --> P1[Phase A Coil]
    D --> P2[Phase B Coil]
    D --> P3[Phase C Coil]
    D --> P4[Phase D Coil]
    P1 --> RT[Rotor with N_r Teeth]
    P2 --> RT
    P3 --> RT
    P4 --> RT
    RT --> SH[Shaft - Discrete Steps]

    style S1 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d
    style D fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    style P1 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
    style P2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
    style P3 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
    style P4 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
    style RT fill:#d1d5db,stroke:#374151
    style SH fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
```

**Step Sequencing Table (Full-step, 2-phase-on mode):**

| Step | Phase A | Phase B | Phase C | Phase D | Rotor Angle |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | $0^{\circ}$ |
| 2 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | $\theta_s$ |
| 3 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | $2\theta_s$ |
| 4 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | $3\theta_s$ |
| 5 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | $4\theta_s = 360^{\circ}$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define an actuator in the context of IoT. List any three commonly used actuators in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer:**
An actuator is a device that converts an electrical control signal into a physical action such as motion, heat, light, or fluid flow. In IoT, actuators execute the "act" stage of the sense–decide–act paradigm.

Three common actuators are:
1. DC Motor — for rotary motion (e.g., smart fan).
2. Solenoid Valve — for fluid control (e.g., smart irrigation).
3. Stepper Motor — for precise angular positioning (e.g., 3D printer).

*(Full credit: definition = 1 mark, three examples = 2 marks)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between an Electromechanical Relay (EMR) and a Solid State Relay (SSR) based on: (i) switching element, (ii) lifetime, (iii) switching speed.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | EMR | SSR |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Switching element | Electromagnetic + mechanical contacts | Semiconductor (TRIAC / MOSFET) |
| Lifetime | $10^5$ to $10^6$ operations | $>10^9$ operations |
| Switching speed | $5 - 15$ ms | $0.1 - 1$ ms |

*(1 mark each criterion — three crisp points expected.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a DC motor with a neat block diagram. Derive the expressions for **(i)** armature torque and **(ii)** back-EMF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole DC shunt motor has 600 armature conductors, wave-wound (so $A=2$), with flux per pole $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb. If the armature current is 80 A and the speed is 1500 RPM, find the **(i)** back-EMF, **(ii)** armature torque, and **(iii)** mechanical power developed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

1. **Construction (2 marks):**
   - Stator (field) — produces steady magnetic flux $\Phi$ per pole.
   - Rotor (armature) — Z conductors wound on a laminated core, mounted on shaft.
   - Commutator + brushes — reverse armature current every half-turn to maintain unidirectional torque.

2. **Working principle (1 mark):**
   When DC supply is given, current in the armature conductors and the magnetic field of the stator interact via the Lorentz force $F = BIL$, producing a torque that rotates the rotor.

3. **Derivation — Armature Torque (2 marks):**
   Current per conductor $I_{cond} = I_a/A$.
   Force on each conductor $F_{cond} = \Phi I_a L/(aA)$.
   Torque per conductor $T_{cond} = F_{cond} \cdot r$.
   Total torque: $T_a = Z T_{cond} = (ZLr/aA) \Phi I_a$.
   Hence $\boxed{T_a = k_t \Phi I_a}$.

4. **Derivation — Back-EMF (2 marks):**
   Flux cuts per conductor per revolution = $\Phi P$.
   Flux cuts per conductor per second = $\Phi P N$.
   EMF per conductor = $\Phi P N$.
   Total back-EMF: $E_b = (Z/A) \Phi P N$.
   Hence $\boxed{E_b = k_e \Phi N}$.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:** $P = 4$ poles, $Z = 600$ conductors, wave-wound $\Rightarrow A = 2$, $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb, $I_a = 80$ A, $N = 1500$ RPM $= 1500/60 = 25$ rev/s.

**(i) Back-EMF:**

$$E_b = \frac{Z \cdot \Phi \cdot P \cdot N}{A} = \frac{600 \times 0.05 \times 4 \times 25}{2}$$

$$E_b = \frac{3000}{2} = 1500 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Armature Torque:**

$$T_a = \frac{Z \cdot \Phi \cdot P \cdot I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{600 \times 0.05 \times 4 \times 80}{2\pi \times 2}$$

$$T_a = \frac{9600}{4\pi} = \frac{2400}{\pi} \approx 763.94 \text{ Nm}$$

**(iii) Mechanical Power Developed:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P_{mech} = T_a \cdot \omega = 763.94 \times 50\pi \approx 763.94 \times 157.08$$

$$P_{mech} \approx 120{,}000 \text{ W} = 120 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification via $P = E_b I_a$:** $1500 \times 80 = 120{,}000$ W ✓ *(matches, confirming the conservation-of-energy identity $T \omega = E_b I_a$).*

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a stepper motor. Derive the expression for the step angle and compute the shaft speed for a 200-step-per-revolution motor driven at 1 kHz pulse rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a Solid State Relay (SSR) with a block diagram. Compare its performance with an Electromechanical Relay (EMR). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

1. **Definition & Construction (2 marks):**
   - A stepper motor is a brushless DC motor that rotates in discrete angular steps.
   - Stator: toothed phase windings (commonly 2 or 4 phases).
   - Rotor: toothed permanent magnet or variable-reluctance iron core.
   - The stator and rotor tooth counts determine the step resolution.

2. **Working Principle (2 marks):**
   - Each phase, when energized, magnetically attracts the nearest rotor tooth, locking it in position.
   - Sequential energization of phases rotates the rotor by one tooth-pitch each time a new phase is energized.
   - Direction is reversed by reversing the phase sequence.
   - Position is determined by counting pulses — no feedback encoder required (open-loop).

3. **Derivation (2 marks):**
   For a variable-reluctance stepper with $N_r$ rotor teeth:

   $$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N_r}$$

   **Given:** 200 steps/rev $\Rightarrow \theta_s = 360^{\circ}/200 = 1.8^{\circ}$.
   Pulse rate $f_p = 1000$ Hz.

   $$\omega = \theta_s \cdot f_p = 1.8^{\circ} \times 1000 = 1800^{\circ}/\text{s}$$

   $$\omega = \frac{1800^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} = 5 \text{ rev/s} = 300 \text{ RPM}$$

4. **Answer (1 mark):** $\boxed{\theta_s = 1.8^{\circ}, \text{ speed} = 300 \text{ RPM}}$

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

1. **Construction (3 marks):**
   - **Input LED (Infrared):** Converts the input DC control signal to light.
   - **Opto-coupler:** Provides galvanic isolation between input and output (signal passes via light, not electricity).
   - **Zero-cross detector:** Senses AC mains zero voltage crossing for minimum-EMI switching.
   - **Output switching element:** TRIAC (for AC) or MOSFET/IGBT (for DC).

2. **Working (2 marks):**
   - Input current → LED emits IR light → phototriac receives light → generates gate signal → TRIAC conducts at next zero-cross → load energized.
   - All-electronic, no moving parts, silent, fast.

3. **Comparison with EMR (2 marks):**
   | Parameter | SSR | EMR |
   |:--|:--|:--|
   | Lifetime | $>10^9$ ops | $10^5 - 10^6$ ops |
   | Speed | $\mu$s | ms |
   | Noise | Silent | Click |
   | Wear | None | Contact bounce/wear |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $k_e$ and $k_t$:** Many students write back-EMF as $E_b = k_t \Phi I_a$. This is wrong — it is the *torque* equation that uses $I_a$. Back-EMF uses $\omega$ (angular velocity) or $N$ (RPM).
>
> 2. **Missing units:** Always write the unit (Nm, V, rad/s, A). Marks are deducted if units are absent in Part B numerical answers.
>
> 3. **Solenoid force formula sign:** A common error is to forget that force is *attractive* — i.e., directed to *reduce* the air-gap. Hence the negative gradient of energy with respect to $g$ is positive in the *pulling* direction.
>
> 4. **Stepper step angle:** Students sometimes use $N_s$ (stator teeth) instead of $N_r$ (rotor teeth). For variable-reluctance steppers, the formula is **$360/N_r$**, not $360/N_s$.
>
> 5. **SSR vs EMR switch-off behavior:** Students forget that an SSR has a small off-state leakage current ($1-10$ mA). For completely off applications, EMR is preferable.
>
> 6. **No mention of commutation in DC motor:** Many students skip explaining the role of the **commutator** in reversing current direction. Without it, the rotor would oscillate, not rotate continuously.
>
> 7. **Solenoid valve vs solenoid:** A solenoid is just the coil-and-plunger assembly. A solenoid *valve* adds the valve body, seat, and spring — do not interchange the terms.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Actuator = "muscle" of IoT**, converting digital commands into physical action. Without actuators, IoT is just a monitoring system.
- **DC Motor** → continuous rotary motion, governed by Lorentz force ($F = BIL$), torque ($T = k_t \Phi I_a$), and back-EMF ($E_b = k_e \Phi \omega$).
- **Solenoid Valve** → uses electromagnetic force to pull a plunger that opens/closes a fluid path; force $F = \mu_0 N^2 I^2 A / (2 g^2)$, falls as $1/g^2$.
- **EMR (Electromechanical Relay)** → electromechanical switch using a coil-driven armature; mechanical contacts, audible click, limited lifetime ($10^5-10^6$ ops), slow ($5-15$ ms).
- **SSR (Solid State Relay)** → electronic switch using LED + opto-coupler + TRIAC/MOSFET; silent, very fast, long life ($>10^9$ ops), zero-cross switching for low EMI.
- **Stepper Motor** → discrete angular steps, $\theta_s = 360^{\circ}/N_r$, open-loop position by counting pulses, used in 3D printers / CNC / gimbals.
- **EMR vs SSR:** EMR has on-state $\approx 0$ V drop but wear-prone; SSR has $1-2$ V drop but wear-free.
- **Speed control of DC motor** in IoT is done by **PWM** from the MCU (e.g., ESP32 LEDC peripheral).
- **Galvanic isolation** is provided by *both* EMR (magnetic) and SSR (optical), ensuring the low-voltage MCU is safe from high-voltage load transients.
- **Holding torque** in steppers drops as $f_p^2$ increases — so high speeds reduce torque.
- **Zero-cross switching** in AC SSRs reduces inrush current and RFI.
- **Solenoid valves are categorized** as Normally Closed (NC) or Normally Open (NO).
- **Back-EMF in a DC motor opposes supply** — it is the reason the armature current self-regulates (at steady state, $V - E_b = I_a R_a$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU Module 2, expect at least one **14-mark question** comparing EMR vs SSR or DC motor derivations. Memorize formulas (4), (7), (8), and (9) from the formula sheet and always carry units through every calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Boards - Overview of Typical Microcontroller and Single Board Computers Used in IoT Applications - Arduino Uno, ESP32, and Raspberry Pi (Overview Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET785 - INTERNET OF THINGS BASED SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Embedded Boards - Overview of Typical Microcontroller and Single Board Computers Used in IoT Applications - Arduino Uno, ESP32, and Raspberry Pi (Overview Only) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Embedded Boards for IoT An **Embedded Board** in the context of IoT is a compact, self-contained computing platform that integrates a processor/microcontroller, memory, and input/output peripherals on a single Printed Circuit Board (PCB) to execute dedicated control, sensing, or communication tasks within an Internet of Things ecosystem. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** The syllabus explicitly categorizes IoT edge devices into two broad hardware classes: > 1. **Microcontroller Boards** (e.g., Arduino Uno, ESP32) — firmware-driven, deterministic, real-time. > 2. **Single Board Computers (SBCs)** (e.g., Raspberry Pi) — OS-driven, high-throughput, multipurpose. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Body's Nervous System vs. Brain" Model:** > - **Arduino Uno** behaves like a **spinal reflex arc** — extremely fast, single-purpose, reacts directly to a stimulus (sensor) without thinking. Great for blinking LEDs or reading temperature. > - **ESP32** is like a **modern smartphone's wireless chip with a brain** — it can sense, react, and *also* talk to the internet (Wi-Fi/Bluetooth) on its own. Perfect for a smart light switch. > - **Raspberry Pi** acts like a **full desktop computer squeezed onto a credit card** — it can run multiple programs, display video, host a webpage, and even do AI — all at once. Ideal for a smart doorbell with facial recognition. ### 1.3 The "Three Pillars" of IoT Embedded Hardware | Board | Primary Class | Operating System | Clock Speed | Core Architectural Philosophy | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Arduino Uno** | 8-bit Microcontroller Board | None (Bare-metal firmware) | **16 MHz** | Simple, deterministic, real-time I/O control | | **ESP32** | 32-bit Microcontroller (SoC) | None / RTOS (FreeRTOS) | **up to 240 MHz** | Wireless-connected edge intelligence | | **Raspberry Pi** | Single Board Computer (SBC) | Linux (Raspberry Pi OS) | **up to 2.4 GHz** | General-purpose edge gateway / server | ### 1.4 Key Terminology Map > [!IMPORTANT] > - **MCU (Microcontroller Unit):** A single IC containing CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O. Examples: ATmega328P (Arduino), Xtensa LX6 (ESP32). > - **SoC (System on Chip):** A higher-integration IC that combines MCU/MPU + wireless radios + peripherals. Example: ESP32. > - **SBC (Single Board Computer):** A complete computer on one board, capable of running a full OS. Example: Raspberry Pi 4/5. > - **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output):** Physical pins used to read sensors or drive actuators. > - **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Hardware that converts analog voltage levels into digital numbers (e.g., 0–5V → 0–1023). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix ## 2.1 The Architectural Divide: Microcontroller vs. Single Board Computer The most critical conceptual distinction a KTU examiner expects you to know is the **architectural philosophy** behind each class. ### 2.1.1 Microcontroller Boards (Arduino Uno, ESP32) A microcontroller board is designed around the **Harvard architecture** (separate program and data memory buses) and runs a single loop of firmware (often written in C/C++ via the Arduino framework). - **Deterministic Execution:** Predictable timing; the time taken to execute any line of code is known in advance. - **Low Power:** Typically draws **mW to a few hundred mW**, suitable for battery-operated nodes. - **Bare-Metal Boot:** No operating system overhead; the firmware starts executing within microseconds of power-on. - **Real-Time Capability:** Can guarantee response times in the **microsecond (μs)** range — vital for motor control and sensor polling. ### 2.1.2 Single Board Computers (Raspberry Pi) A Raspberry Pi uses a **Broadcom SoC with an ARM Cortex-A series CPU** — essentially the same architecture family used in modern smartphones. - **Von Neumann Architecture (modified):** Unified memory space for instructions and data. - **OS-Mediated:** Requires a full Linux kernel, which means **non-deterministic** timing. - **High Throughput:** Capable of running complex stacks — Python interpreters, web servers, database engines, even machine learning inference. - **Boot Time:** Typically **10–30 seconds** from power-on to user-space. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet (Exam "Cheat-Sheet" Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the single most important table to memorize for KTU Module 2.** A question comparing any two of these three boards is a recurring pattern in the 2024 Scheme question papers. | Parameter | Arduino Uno (R3) | ESP32 (DevKit V1) | Raspberry Pi 4 Model B | |---|---|---|---| | **Class** | 8-bit MCU Board | 32-bit Wireless SoC Board | Single Board Computer | | **Main IC** | ATmega328P | Espressif ESP32 (Xtensa LX6 dual-core) | Broadcom BCM2711 (Cortex-A72 quad-core) | | **CPU Architecture** | AVR (8-bit, RISC) | Xtensa LX6 (32-bit, RISC) | ARM Cortex-A72 (64-bit) | | **Clock Speed** | **16 MHz** | **80 / 160 / 240 MHz** (configurable) | **1.5 / 1.8 GHz** (1.5–2.4 GHz on Pi 5) | | **Operating Voltage** | **5 V** | **3.3 V** | **3.3 V logic, 5 V supply** | | **SRAM / RAM** | 2 KB | 520 KB | 1 / 2 / 4 / 8 GB LPDDR4 | | **Flash Storage** | 32 KB (program) | 4 MB (typical SPI flash) | microSD card (up to 1 TB) | | **GPIO Pins** | 14 digital (6 PWM) + 6 analog | 34 digital (many PWM) + 18 ADC channels | 40 (28 GPIO) | | **ADC Resolution** | 10-bit (1024 levels) | 12-bit (4096 levels) | No native ADC on Pi 4 (external required) | | **Wireless** | **None** (needs shield) | **Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n + Bluetooth 4.2/BLE** | **None** (needs USB dongle) | | **Onboard OS** | None (bare firmware) | None (or FreeRTOS) | Raspberry Pi OS (Linux) | | **Programming Language** | C/C++ (Arduino IDE) | C/C++, MicroPython, Arduino | Python, C/C++, Java, Node.js, etc. | | **Power Consumption** | **~50 mA** (~250 mW) | **~80–240 mA** (~0.4–1.2 W Wi-Fi active) | **~3 A @ 5 V** (~15 W full load) | | **Typical IoT Role** | Sensor node, motor control | Wi-Fi/BLE edge node, smart device | Edge gateway, data aggregator, AI | ## 2.3 Power & Energy Math — Quick Reference For KTU problems on energy budgets of IoT nodes, the standard power formulas apply: $$P = V \times I$$ $$E = P \times t = V \times I \times t$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** In a wireless sensor network (WSN) powered by a **2000 mAh @ 3.7 V Li-Po battery**, an Arduino Uno node may last **6–12 months**, an ESP32 with duty-cycled Wi-Fi **2–8 weeks**, while a Raspberry Pi gateway needs mains power or a large solar panel — making board selection a *direct function* of the deployment scenario. ## 2.4 Arduino Uno — Detailed Anatomy - **Microcontroller IC:** ATmega328P (Atmel/Microchip, 8-bit AVR RISC). - **Bootloader:** Optiboot (~0.5 KB) enables USB-based flashing. - **USB Interface:** ATmega16U2 (or CH340 on clones) acts as a USB-to-Serial bridge. - **Power Regulation:** Onboard LDO (AMS1117-5.0 and LM358) steps down external 7–12 V to stable 5 V and 3.3 V rails. - **Shield Ecosystem:** Stackable daughter boards (e.g., Ethernet Shield, Motor Driver Shield) extend functionality without soldering. ## 2.5 ESP32 — Detailed Anatomy - **SoC:** Tensilica Xtensa LX6 **dual-core** (or single-core in low-end variants). - **Wireless Co-existence:** Integrated 2.4 GHz radio handles Wi-Fi station + access point + BLE simultaneously. - **Touch Sensing:** 10 capacitive touch GPIOs enable touch-based UI without mechanical buttons. - **Crypto Engine:** Hardware acceleration for AES, SHA-2, RSA — important for IoT security. - **Variants:** ESP32-S3 (AI acceleration), ESP32-C3 (RISC-V, single core), ESP32-H2 (Thread/Zigbee). ## 2.6 Raspberry Pi — Detailed Anatomy - **SoC:** Broadcom BCM2711 (Pi 4) / BCM2712 (Pi 5). - **GPU:** VideoCore VI (Pi 4) / VideoCore VII (Pi 5) — supports H.265 4K decode. - **Connectivity:** 2× USB 3.0, 2× USB 2.0, Gigabit Ethernet, 2× micro-HDMI, CSI/DSI camera/display ports. - **GPIO Compatibility:** 40-pin header is software-compatible with Pi 1 Model B+ onwards, enabling HAT (Hardware Attached on Top) modules. - **IoT Use Cases:** Runs **MQTT brokers** (Mosquitto), **Node-RED** (visual flow programming), and **TensorFlow Lite** (edge ML). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Selection Logic > [!NOTE] > Since this is an **"Overview Only"** topic per the KTU 2024 syllabus, the analytical depth here focuses on **selection rationale, architectural derivations, and quantitative board-comparison logic** rather than circuit derivations. ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: How a GPIO Pin "Talks" to a Sensor To ground the abstract concept, let us walk through *how an Arduino Uno reads a temperature sensor* and contrast it with a Raspberry Pi reading the same sensor. ### Step 1 — Physical Layer (Voltage Domain) Both boards use **3.3 V CMOS logic** on their ADC/GPIO pins. A TMP36 temperature sensor outputs an analog voltage: $$V_{out} = 500 \text{ mV} + 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T$$ ### Step 2 — Sampling (ADC Resolution) The ADC converts this analog voltage to a discrete digital integer. The number of distinct levels is given by: $$N_{levels} = 2^{n}$$ where $n$ is the ADC resolution in bits. - For **Arduino Uno (n = 10):** $$N_{levels} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ distinct levels}$$ - For **ESP32 (n = 12):** $$N_{levels} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ distinct levels}$$ ### Step 3 — Quantization Step (LSB Size) The **Least Significant Bit (LSB) voltage** determines the smallest voltage change detectable: $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$ Assuming $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$: - **Arduino Uno (10-bit):** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3}{1023} \approx 3.226 \text{ mV per step}$$ - **ESP32 (12-bit):** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 0.806 \text{ mV per step}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Conclusion:** The ESP32 resolves **4× finer voltage changes** than the Arduino Uno, allowing it to detect **smaller temperature deltas** from the same sensor. ### Step 4 — Conversion to Engineering Units (Temperature) For the TMP36 sensor at room temperature ($T = 25 \text{ °C}$), expected $V_{out} = 750 \text{ mV}$. - On Arduino (10-bit): $ADC = \dfrac{750 \text{ mV}}{3.226 \text{ mV/step}} \approx 232 \text{ counts}$ - On ESP32 (12-bit): $ADC = \dfrac{750 \text{ mV}}{0.806 \text{ mV/step}} \approx 930 \text{ counts}$ The **reverse mapping** (counts → temperature) is then: $$T = \frac{V_{out} - 500 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$ $$T = \frac{(ADC \times V_{LSB}) - 500 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}}$$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Which Board for Which IoT Workload? | IoT Workload Scenario | Recommended Board | Justification (Quantitative) | |---|---|---| | Battery-powered soil moisture sensor sending data every 10 min via Wi-Fi | **ESP32 (deep-sleep mode)** | Sleep current ~ **10 μA**, wakes via timer, transmits, sleeps — months of battery life. | | High-speed motor control loop (PID @ 1 kHz) for a CNC pen plotter | **Arduino Uno (or Nano)** | Deterministic loop, no OS jitter, ISR latency < **5 μs**. | | Edge AI camera (Person Detection) processing 1080p video | **Raspberry Pi 4/5** | Needs **>4 GB RAM**, Linux drivers for camera, can run TensorFlow Lite. | | Simple LED blinking + push-button input for a kids' toy | **Arduino Uno** | Cheapest (~₹400), simplest IDE, no OS needed. | | Smart home hub aggregating MQTT messages from 50 sensors | **Raspberry Pi 4** | Runs Mosquitto broker + Node-RED + InfluxDB simultaneously. | | Wearable BLE heart-rate monitor with OLED display | **ESP32 (with BLE + integrated touch)** | Tiny form factor, BLE consumes < 1 mW avg, on-board touch sensors for UI. | ## 3.3 The "Power-Performance Trade-off" Derivation A useful KTU-style analytical result is the **Energy per Instruction (EPI)** approximation, useful for comparing nodes: $$EPI \approx \frac{P \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$$ where: - $P$ = average power in Watts - $CPI$ = cycles per instruction (avg) ≈ 1 for modern RISC - $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency in Hz A representative comparison (rule-of-thumb figures): | Board | $f_{clk}$ (Hz) | $P$ (W) | $EPI$ (J/instruction, order of magnitude) | |---|---|---|---| | Arduino Uno | $1.6 \times 10^{7}$ | $0.25$ | $\sim 1.6 \times 10^{-8}$ | | ESP32 (idle Wi-Fi off) | $8.0 \times 10^{7}$ | $0.08$ | $\sim 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | | Raspberry Pi 4 (idle) | $1.5 \times 10^{9}$ | $3.0$ | $\sim 2.0 \times 10^{-9}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Despite being 90× faster, the ESP32 is **more energy-efficient per instruction** than the Arduino when Wi-Fi is off, because the AVR is 8-bit with 16 MHz and the LX6 is 32-bit superscalar. This is the **real reason** the ESP32 has displaced the Arduino in modern IoT. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level IoT Edge Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Cloud[("Cloud Platform
AWS IoT / Azure IoT Hub")] Gateway["Edge Gateway
Raspberry Pi 4
Runs MQTT Broker Node-RED"] N1["Sensor Node 1
ESP32 + DHT22
Wi-Fi MQTT"] N2["Sensor Node 2
Arduino Uno + Ethernet Shield"] N3["Actuator Node
ESP32 + Relay
BLE Control"] Sensors["Physical Sensors
Temperature Humidity Motion"] Actuators["Physical Actuators
Relay Motor Buzzer"] Cloud <--> Gateway Gateway <--> N1 Gateway <--> N2 N1 <--> Sensors N3 <--> Actuators N3 <--> N1 ``` ## 4.2 Arduino Uno — Board-Level Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR USB["USB Type B
Power and Programming"] Serial["ATmega16U2
USB to Serial Bridge"] MCU["ATmega328P
CPU 16 MHz
Flash 32 KB
SRAM 2 KB
EEPROM 1 KB"] Power["Power Jack 7 to 12 V
AMS1117 LDO Regulators"] Analog["6x Analog Input Pins
10 bit ADC"] Digital["14x Digital Pins
6 PWM capable"] I2C["I2C Bus
SDA SCL"] SPI["SPI Bus
MOSI MISO SCK"] UART["UART
TX RX"] USB --> Serial --> MCU Power --> MCU MCU --> Analog MCU --> Digital MCU --> I2C MCU --> SPI MCU --> UART ``` ## 4.3 ESP32 — SoC Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB Antenna["2.4 GHz Antenna
on PCB Trace"] Radio["Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n
Bluetooth Classic
BLE 4.2"] CPU1["Xtensa LX6 Core 0
240 MHz"] CPU2["Xtensa LX6 Core 1
240 MHz"] ROM["448 KB ROM"] SRAM["520 KB SRAM"] RTC["RTC Memory 8 KB
Deep Sleep Retention"] Crypto["Hardware Crypto
AES SHA RSA"] Touch["10x Capacitive Touch"] PWM["16x PWM Channels"] ADC2["2x 12 bit SAR ADC
18 Channels"] Radio <--> CPU1 Radio <--> CPU2 CPU1 <--> ROM CPU1 <--> SRAM CPU1 <--> RTC CPU1 <--> Crypto CPU1 <--> Touch CPU1 <--> PWM CPU1 <--> ADC2 CPU2 <--> SRAM CPU2 <--> ADC2 CPU2 <--> PWM ``` ## 4.4 Raspberry Pi 4 — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB SD["microSD Card Slot
OS and Storage"] SOC["Broadcom BCM2711
4x ARM Cortex A72 1.5 GHz
VideoCore VI GPU"] RAM["LPDDR4 RAM
1 2 4 or 8 GB"] USB3["2x USB 3.0"] USB2["2x USB 2.0"] ETH["Gigabit Ethernet"] HDMI["2x micro HDMI
4K at 60 Hz"] GPIO["40 pin GPIO Header
UART I2C SPI I2S PWM"] CSI["CSI Camera Port"] DSI["DSI Display Port"] PWR["USB C 5 V at 3 A"] SD --> SOC SOC --> RAM SOC --> USB3 SOC --> USB2 SOC --> ETH SOC --> HDMI SOC --> GPIO SOC --> CSI SOC --> DSI PWR --> SOC ``` ## 4.5 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right IoT Board ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Start Need a board for IoT"]) Q1{"Does it need
a full OS
or display"} Q2{"Does it need
Wi Fi or Bluetooth
on board"} Q3{"Is it battery powered
and simple"} Pi["Raspberry Pi 4 or 5"] Esp["ESP32 DevKit"] Ar["Arduino Uno or Nano"] Start --> Q1 Q1 -- Yes --> Pi Q1 -- No --> Q2 Q2 -- Yes --> Esp Q2 -- No --> Q3 Q3 -- Yes --> Ar Q3 -- No --> Esp ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Takeaway (KTU Revision Hook):** Memorize the four diagrams above. In Module 2 viva / 14-mark questions, examiners frequently ask students to *draw and label* the block diagram of an Arduino Uno, ESP32, or Raspberry Pi. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > > **Q1.** Differentiate between a **microcontroller board** and a **single board computer** with one suitable example each. > > **Model Answer:** > - A **microcontroller board** integrates a single-chip microcontroller (CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O) on a PCB and runs **bare-metal firmware** (e.g., Arduino Uno). It is designed for **deterministic, real-time control tasks** and has limited resources. > - A **single board computer (SBC)** integrates a microprocessor (ARM Cortex-A class), RAM, storage interfaces, and full OS support on one board (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4). It runs a **full Linux OS** and is suited for **multitasking, networking, and complex applications**. > - Key difference: MCUs have **no OS, low power (mW), and μs response**; SBCs have **OS, high power (W), and ms response**. > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > > **Q2.** List **four** key features of the **ESP32** that make it suitable for IoT applications. > > **Model Answer:** > 1. **Integrated 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) + Bluetooth/BLE** — eliminates need for external radio module. > 2. **Dual-core Xtensa LX6 32-bit processor up to 240 MHz** — sufficient compute for edge logic. > 3. **Ultra-low-power deep-sleep mode (~10 μA)** — enables battery-powered deployments for months. > 4. **Integrated peripherals** — 12-bit ADC, 10 capacitive touch sensors, hardware crypto engine (AES/SHA), 4 MB flash, 520 KB SRAM. > 5. (Bonus) **Arduino IDE / MicroPython support** — easy entry for beginners. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) > ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture of Arduino Uno** with the help of a neat block diagram. List its important specifications. > > **(b)** **[7 Marks]** Compare Arduino Uno and Raspberry Pi 4 in terms of **processor, memory, power, OS, wireless capability, and typical IoT use case** in a tabular form. > > #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] > > **Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.2 — Arduino Uno Board Block Diagram) > > **Architecture Explanation:** > 1. **Microcontroller IC:** ATmega328P — 8-bit AVR RISC, 16 MHz clock, 32 KB Flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM. > 2. **USB Interface:** ATmega16U2 converts USB to serial UART, enabling PC-to-board communication and programming. > 3. **Power Section:** External 7–12 V DC via barrel jack is regulated by AMS1117-5.0 to 5 V and further to 3.3 V for shields. > 4. **Digital I/O:** 14 pins (of which 6 support PWM) for digital read/write. > 5. **Analog I/O:** 6 pins connected to a 10-bit SAR ADC; reference voltage 5 V default. > 6. **Communication Buses:** I2C (TWI), SPI, UART exposed on dedicated pins. > 7. **Reset Circuit:** Automatic reset on USB enumeration enables sketch upload without manual button press. > 8. **ICSP Header:** Allows low-level programming of ATmega328P bypassing the bootloader. > > **Key Specifications (write in exam):** > | Parameter | Value | > |---|---| > | MCU | ATmega328P | > | Architecture | 8-bit AVR RISC | > | Clock | 16 MHz | > | Flash | 32 KB | > | SRAM | 2 KB | > | EEPROM | 1 KB | > | Digital I/O | 14 (6 PWM) | > | Analog Inputs | 6 (10-bit ADC) | > | Operating Voltage | 5 V | > | Input Voltage (recommended) | 7–12 V | > > #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] > > **Comparison Table:** > > | Parameter | Arduino Uno | Raspberry Pi 4 | > |---|---|---| > | Processor | ATmega328P, 8-bit AVR, 16 MHz | Broadcom BCM2711, 64-bit ARM Cortex-A72 quad-core, 1.5 GHz | > | RAM | 2 KB SRAM | 1–8 GB LPDDR4 | > | OS | None (bare firmware) | Raspberry Pi OS (Linux) | > | Wireless | None (needs shield) | None (needs USB dongle) | > | Power | ~250 mW | ~15 W (full load) | > | Typical IoT Use | Simple sensor node, motor control | Edge gateway, MQTT broker, AI inference | > > **Conclusion (write 1–2 lines):** Arduino Uno suits low-power, real-time, single-task IoT nodes; Raspberry Pi 4 suits complex edge gateways requiring an OS and multitasking. > ### **Question B (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a)** **[7 Marks]** With a block diagram, describe the **internal architecture and key features of the ESP32** SoC. Why is it preferred over Arduino Uno for wireless IoT nodes? > > **(b)** **[7 Marks]** A wireless sensor node uses an **ESP32 running on a 3.7 V, 2000 mAh Li-Po battery**. In **active mode (Wi-Fi TX)**, the average current is **240 mA**; in **deep-sleep**, it is **10 μA**. If the node wakes, transmits for **3 seconds**, and sleeps for **57 seconds** in each cycle, calculate the **average current** and **battery lifetime in days**. Assume 80% depth-of-discharge (DoD) limit. > > #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] > > **Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.3 — ESP32 SoC Block Diagram) > > **Architecture Explanation:** > 1. **CPU Cores:** Dual-core Tensilica Xtensa LX6, 32-bit, up to 240 MHz. > 2. **Memory:** 448 KB ROM (bootloader), 520 KB SRAM, 4 MB SPI flash (typical module), 8 KB RTC fast memory for sleep retention. > 3. **Wireless Radio:** Integrated 2.4 GHz — Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n (station + AP + promiscuous), Bluetooth v4.2 BR/EDR, BLE. > 4. **Peripherals:** 2× 12-bit SAR ADCs (18 channels), 2× DAC, 16 PWM channels, 4× SPI, 2× I2C, 3× UART, 10 capacitive touch GPIOs, CAN 2.0, I2S, Ethernet MAC. > 5. **Security Engine:** Hardware-accelerated AES-128/256, SHA-2, RSA, secure boot, flash encryption. > 6. **Power Modes:** Active, Modem-sleep, Light-sleep, Deep-sleep (RTC-only) — current ranges from **~80 mA (active)** down to **~10 μA (deep-sleep)**. > > **Why ESP32 over Arduino Uno for wireless IoT:** > - **Onboard radio** — Arduino Uno requires an external Wi-Fi shield (e.g., ESP-01) wired via UART/SPI, increasing BOM cost and PCB complexity. > - **32-bit vs. 8-bit** — 32-bit operations in one cycle vs. multiple cycles on AVR. > - **Higher clock** — 240 MHz vs. 16 MHz, enabling on-board protocol stacks (MQTT, TLS). > - **Lower sleep current** — 10 μA enables year-long battery deployments. > > #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] > > **Given:** > - $I_{active} = 240 \text{ mA}$ for $t_{active} = 3 \text{ s}$ > - $I_{sleep} = 10 \text{ μA} = 0.01 \text{ mA}$ for $t_{sleep} = 57 \text{ s}$ > - Total cycle: $T = 3 + 57 = 60 \text{ s}$ > - Battery: $C = 2000 \text{ mAh}$ at $V = 3.7 \text{ V}$ > - DoD limit = 80%, so usable capacity $C_{usable} = 0.8 \times 2000 = 1600 \text{ mAh}$ > > **Step 1 — Average current using weighted mean (duty cycle):** > > $$I_{avg} = \frac{(I_{active} \times t_{active}) + (I_{sleep} \times t_{sleep})}{T}$$ > > $$I_{avg} = \frac{(240 \times 3) + (0.01 \times 57)}{60}$$ > > $$I_{avg} = \frac{720 + 0.57}{60}$$ > > $$I_{avg} = \frac{720.57}{60} \approx 12.01 \text{ mA}$$ > > **Step 2 — Battery lifetime in hours:** > > $$T_{hours} = \frac{C_{usable}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{1600 \text{ mAh}}{12.01 \text{ mA}} \approx 133.2 \text{ hours}$$ > > **Step 3 — Convert to days:** > > $$T_{days} = \frac{133.2}{24} \approx 5.55 \text{ days}$$ > > **Conclusion:** Under this duty cycle, the node lasts only **~5.5 days**. To extend life, the engineer should **reduce active time** (faster transmission, MQTT-SN, batched payloads) or **increase sleep duration** (e.g., 5 min interval instead of 1 min). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the duty cycle:** Many students compute the lifetime using $2000 / 240 = 8.3$ hours, which is wrong — the node spends most of its life asleep! > 2. **Ignoring the DoD limit:** Lithium batteries degrade if discharged below 20% — always apply the **80% DoD rule** for Li-Po. > 3. **Unit errors:** Mixing μA and mA is the #1 calculation error in KTU answer sheets. Convert to a **single unit (mA)** before computing. > 4. **Confusing GPIO voltage with supply voltage:** Arduino Uno logic is **5 V**, ESP32/RPi logic is **3.3 V** — connecting a 5 V sensor output to ESP32 GPIO will **damage the pin**. Always check voltage compatibility. > 5. **Forgetting that Raspberry Pi has no native ADC:** Students often write "Pi 4 has 6 analog pins" — this is wrong; the Pi 4 requires an **external ADC chip (e.g., MCP3008)** via SPI. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 2 — Embedded Boards (Overview): Final Revision Bullets** > > ✅ **Three Boards to Master:** Arduino Uno (8-bit MCU), ESP32 (32-bit wireless SoC), Raspberry Pi 4/5 (SBC). > > ✅ **Core Distinction:** MCU boards = no OS, real-time, low power, single-task. SBC = full OS, multitasking, high power. > > ✅ **Arduino Uno:** ATmega328P, 16 MHz, 32 KB Flash, 2 KB SRAM, 14 digital + 6 analog pins, **10-bit ADC**, **5 V logic**, no wireless. > > ✅ **ESP32:** Xtensa LX6 **dual-core**, **240 MHz**, 520 KB SRAM, **integrated Wi-Fi + BLE**, 12-bit ADC, **3.3 V logic**, deep-sleep **~10 μA**. > > ✅ **Raspberry Pi 4:** Cortex-A72 **quad-core 1.5 GHz**, 1–8 GB LPDDR4 RAM, microSD OS, **no native ADC**, runs Linux, ~15 W power. > > ✅ **Power Formula:** $P = V \times I$; energy $E = P \times t$. > > ✅ **ADC Resolution:** $N_{levels} = 2^{n}$; LSB voltage $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / (2^{n} - 1)$. > > ✅ **Voltage Compatibility Rule:** Never connect 5 V output into 3.3 V input pins (ESP32, RPi) — use a **level shifter** or **voltage divider**. > > ✅ **Selection Rule of Thumb:** Battery + simple + wireless → **ESP32**. Real-time + deterministic + simple → **Arduino Uno**. OS + display + AI + gateway → **Raspberry Pi**. > > ✅ **Bootloader / OS:** Arduino/ESP32 boot in < 1 second (firmware). Raspberry Pi boots in 10–30 seconds (Linux kernel + services). > > ✅ **Programming:** Arduino/ESP32 use **Arduino IDE (C/C++)** or **MicroPython**; Raspberry Pi uses **Python, C, Java, Node.js** etc. > > ✅ **Wireless Reality:** ESP32 has Wi-Fi + BLE; Arduino Uno has **none**; Raspberry Pi has **none** (USB dongle needed). > > ✅ **Exam Trap:** Always mention **clock speed, memory, GPIO count, operating voltage, and wireless capability** in any "explain" question — examiners check for all five.

topics.title: IoT Communication: IoT Device Gateways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET785 - INTERNET OF THINGS BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IoT Communication: IoT Device Gateways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **IoT Device Gateway** (also called an **IoT Gateway** or **Edge Gateway**) is an intelligent intermediary hardware-software system that acts as the **bridge between local IoT devices/sensors (the "Thing" layer) and the cloud/enterprise back-end (the Internet layer)**. It performs **protocol translation, data aggregation, local intelligence (edge computing), device management, and security enforcement** so that heterogeneous, resource-constrained field devices can communicate seamlessly with the wider Internet and cloud services.

In the official KTU/IoT reference model, the gateway sits at the intersection of the **Perception/Edge layer** and the **Network/Cloud layer**, encapsulating the boundary between the **Operational Technology (OT)** world and the **Information Technology (IT)** world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
