# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Detailed Use-Case Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this topic is **conceptual and architectural** (not formula-driven), the "derivations" here are systematic **comparative analyses and decision matrices** rather than algebraic equations. Each step is fully expanded as required by KTU standards.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Choosing the Right Protocol for an IoT Scenario

We formalize the protocol selection process as a multi-criteria decision.

### Step 1: Identify the Network Characteristics

Define the network constraint vector $N$:

$$
N = \{ B, L, P, C, F \}
$$

where:
- $B$ = Available bandwidth (kbps)
- $L$ = Link reliability / packet loss probability
- $P$ = Device power availability (battery vs mains)
- $C$ = Device class (Class 1, 2, or 3 per RFC 7228)
- $F$ = Firewall/NAT traversal requirement

### Step 2: Identify the Application Characteristics

Define the application requirement vector $A$:

$$
A = \{ Q, R, M, T, S \}
$$

where:
- $Q$ = QoS requirement (best-effort, at-least-once, exactly-once)
- $R$ = Real-time push requirement (boolean)
- $M$ = Number of devices (small fleet / large fleet / massive scale)
- $T$ = Latency tolerance (ms, s, min)
- $S$ = Server-side clients (cloud, web, mobile, all three)

### Step 3: Apply Selection Rules

$$
\text{Protocol} = \begin{cases}
\text{CoAP} & \text{if } (C = 1) \lor (B < 50 \text{ kbps}) \lor (L > 5\%) \lor (P = \text{battery}) \\
\text{MQTT} & \text{if } (R = \text{true}) \lor (M > 10^4) \lor (L \text{ intermittent}) \\
\text{REST API} & \text{if } (S = \{\text{web, mobile}\}) \lor (F = \text{strict}) \\
\text{Hybrid} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** The above is a **simplified KTU-friendly heuristic** for exam purposes. Real industry selection involves additional factors like vendor lock-in, existing infrastructure, and team expertise.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Use-Case Walkthroughs (KTU Exam Style)

### Use Case 1: Smart Agriculture with LoRaWAN

**Scenario:** A farm has 500 soil moisture sensors across 50 hectares, each battery-powered, transmitting small packets every 15 minutes over LoRaWAN. A gateway collects data and forwards to a cloud dashboard accessible via a mobile app.

**Step-by-step solution:**

- **Step 1 — Analyze the device class:** Sensors are Class 1 (often 8-bit MCUs, < 32 KiB RAM). Bandwidth is very low (~0.3–50 kbps). Power is battery.
- **Step 2 — Analyze the application:** Periodic telemetry (not real-time push), large fleet, low data rate, requires cloud dashboard.
- **Step 3 — Select protocol at sensor-to-gateway:** **CoAP over UDP** is ideal because the LoRaWAN MAC layer already provides some reliability, and CoAP's small header fits within LoRaWAN's strict duty cycle. Lightweight **CBOR** encoding further reduces payload size.
- **Step 4 — Select protocol at gateway-to-cloud:** **MQTT** is the natural choice because the gateway can publish all sensor data to an MQTT broker (e.g., HiveMQ), and the cloud platform subscribes to relevant topics. The **Pub/Sub model scales** as more gateways come online.
- **Step 5 — Select protocol for mobile app access:** **REST API** is used by the mobile app to query historical data, configure sensor thresholds, and trigger actuator commands (e.g., irrigation valves).

**Final Architecture:** **CoAP (field) → MQTT (gateway-to-cloud) → REST API (app-to-cloud).**

---

### Use Case 2: Connected Healthcare Wearables

**Scenario:** A wearable ECG patch streams heart rate data every second to a hospital monitoring system. Doctors receive real-time alerts on a web dashboard if abnormal rhythms are detected.

**Step-by-step solution:**

- **Step 1 — Analyze the device class:** Wearable is Class 2 (32-bit MCU, ~256 KiB RAM). Has reliable Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) link to a smartphone.
- **Step 2 — Analyze the application:** **Real-time push** critical, low latency (< 5 s), moderate fleet (10,000 patients), requires guaranteed delivery of alerts.
- **Step 3 — Select protocol at patch-to-phone:** **CoAP** over BLE GATT is efficient. The Observe pattern lets the phone subscribe to ECG data changes without polling.
- **Step 4 — Select protocol at phone-to-cloud:** **MQTT** with **QoS 1 (at-least-once)** ensures alerts are not lost. The hospital's monitoring system subscribes to `/hospital/ward/+/patient/+/ecg` and receives all updates.
- **Step 5 — Select protocol for doctor dashboard:** **REST API** + **WebSocket** for real-time visualization. REST for historical queries, WebSocket for live stream push to the browser.

**Why not just MQTT end-to-end?** The patch is power-constrained and cannot maintain a long-lived TCP/MQTT connection. CoAP over BLE is more power-efficient.

---

### Use Case 3: Smart City Street Lighting

**Scenario:** A municipal corporation deploys 10,000 LED streetlights with integrated luminosity and motion sensors. Each light is a 6LoWPAN node connected to a city-wide mesh. The city wants to dim lights at night, brighten them on motion, and report failures.

**Step-by-step solution:**

- **Step 1 — Analyze the network:** 6LoWPAN over IEEE 802.15.4g — constrained, lossy, low bandwidth. Class 1/2 devices.
- **Step 2 — Analyze the application:** Many-to-one (sensors → central) and one-to-many (control commands). **Multicast** is highly desirable (group dim commands).
- **Step 3 — Select protocol:** **CoAP** is the ideal choice. Reasons:
  - UDP multicast natively supported (`CoAP POST` to a multicast group address).
  - Block-wise transfer handles firmware updates (RFC 7959).
  - `Observe` pattern lets the central system get real-time luminosity readings.
  - Low power consumption suits solar-powered lights.
- **Step 4 — Optional: Bridge to cloud via MQTT.** The 6LoWPAN border router can convert CoAP requests into MQTT messages for the city's central SCADA platform.

---

### Use Case 4: Industrial IoT (IIoT) — Factory Floor Monitoring

**Scenario:** A factory has 200 machines, each with vibration, temperature, and pressure sensors. The factory wants predictive maintenance — alerts must be delivered reliably. The system integrates with the existing ERP (SAP).

**Step-by-step solution:**

- **Step 1 — Analyze the application:** Mission-critical, high reliability, integration with enterprise systems.
- **Step 2 — Select protocol at sensor-to-edge-gateway:** **MQTT-SN (Sensor Network)** or **CoAP**, depending on link technology.
- **Step 3 — Select protocol at edge-to-cloud:** **MQTT** with **QoS 2 (exactly-once)** for critical alerts. Broker (e.g., EMQX) clusters across data centers.
- **Step 4 — Select protocol for ERP integration:** **REST API** — SAP exposes OData REST endpoints; the IoT platform calls these to create maintenance work orders automatically.
- **Step 5 — Select protocol for operator dashboard:** **REST API + WebSocket** for real-time visualization.

**Result:** **MQTT (edge-to-cloud) + REST (cloud-to-ERP/dashboard).**

---

### Use Case 5: Smart Home (Consumer IoT)

**Scenario:** A consumer wants to control smart bulbs, thermostats, and cameras via a smartphone app. Devices connect via Wi-Fi.

**Step-by-step solution:**

- **Step 1 — Analyze:** Devices are mains-powered (no battery constraints), reliable Wi-Fi, single home user.
- **Step 2 — Select protocol:** **MQTT** is common (e.g., Home Assistant uses MQTT). Real-time push is essential — user toggles light, light must respond instantly. QoS 1 is sufficient.
- **Step 3 — Cloud integration:** Vendor cloud (e.g., Tuya, Philips Hue) exposes **REST API** to third-party apps (Google Home, Alexa).
- **Step 4 — Matter/Thread devices:** Newer devices use **CoAP** for Thread-based mesh communication (Matter protocol stack).

**Result:** Mixed — MQTT internally, REST externally, CoAP for Thread/Matter.

---

## 3.3 Quantitative Comparison: Header Overhead

To justify the bandwidth advantage of MQTT/CoAP over REST, we calculate the per-message overhead.

### REST/HTTP Minimum Header Size

A minimal HTTP/1.1 GET request over HTTPS has:

$$
\text{HTTP Header} \approx 200 \text{ bytes (request line + headers)}
$$

$$
\text{TLS Record} \approx 30 \text{ bytes (handshake not counted, just record overhead)}
$$

$$
\text{TCP Header} = 20 \text{ bytes (no options)}
$$

$$
\text{IP Header} = 20 \text{ bytes (IPv4, no options)}
$$

$$
\text{Total REST overhead} \approx 200 + 30 + 20 + 20 = 270 \text{ bytes}
$$

### MQTT Minimum Header Size

$$
\text{MQTT Fixed Header} = 2 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{MQTT Variable Header (topic)} = 2 + N \text{ bytes (topic length + topic name)}
$$

$$
\text{TCP Header} = 20 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{IP Header} = 20 \text{ bytes}
$$

For a 10-character topic:

$$
\text{Total MQTT overhead} \approx 2 + 12 + 20 + 20 = 54 \text{ bytes}
$$

### CoAP Minimum Header Size

$$
\text{CoAP Header} = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{UDP Header} = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{IP Header} = 20 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{6LoWPAN compression (estimate)} \approx 4 \text{ bytes (with IPHC)}
$$

$$
\text{Total CoAP overhead} \approx 4 + 8 + 4 = 16 \text{ bytes (with 6LoWPAN)}
$$

### Overhead Reduction Ratio

$$
\text{Overhead Ratio} = \frac{\text{REST overhead}}{\text{CoAP overhead}} = \frac{270}{16} \approx 16.9 \times
$$

$$
\text{Overhead Ratio} = \frac{\text{REST overhead}}{\text{MQTT overhead}} = \frac{270}{54} = 5 \times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** CoAP has **~17x less overhead** than REST in constrained 6LoWPAN networks. MQTT has **~5x less overhead** than REST. This is why MQTT and CoAP are the preferred choice in low-bandwidth IoT scenarios.

---

## 3.4 Summary Use-Case Selection Matrix

| **IoT Use Case** | **Field Protocol** | **Gateway-to-Cloud** | **Client-to-Cloud** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smart Agriculture (LoRaWAN) | CoAP | MQTT | REST API |
| Healthcare Wearables | CoAP (over BLE) | MQTT (QoS 1) | REST + WebSocket |
| Smart Street Lighting | CoAP (multicast) | MQTT | REST |
| IIoT Predictive Maintenance | MQTT-SN / CoAP | MQTT (QoS 2) | REST |
| Consumer Smart Home | MQTT | MQTT | REST |
| Vehicle Fleet Telemetry | MQTT | MQTT | REST |
| Smart Metering | CoAP | MQTT | REST |

## 4.1 MQTT Pub/Sub Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PUB["PUBLISHERS SENSING LAYER"]
        S1["TEMP SENSOR NODE 1"]
        S2["HUMIDITY SENSOR NODE 2"]
        S3["MOTION SENSOR NODE 3"]
    end

    BR["MQTT BROKER HIVE EMQX"]

    subgraph SUB["SUBSCRIBERS APPLICATION LAYER"]
        MOB["MOBILE APP"]
        DASH["WEB DASHBOARD"]
        DB["ANALYTICS DATABASE"]
        AI["ML ANOMALY DETECTION"]
    end

    S1 -- PUBLISH TOPIC HOME LIVINGROOM TEMP --> BR
    S2 -- PUBLISH TOPIC HOME LIVINGROOM HUMIDITY --> BR
    S3 -- PUBLISH TOPIC HOME ENTRANCE MOTION --> BR

    BR -- SUBSCRIBE TOPIC HOME LIVINGROOM TEMP --> MOB
    BR -- SUBSCRIBE TOPIC HOME LIVINGROOM HUMIDITY --> DASH
    BR -- SUBSCRIBE TOPIC HOME LIVINGROOM TEMP --> DB
    BR -- SUBSCRIBE TOPIC HOME ENTRANCE MOTION --> AI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Sensors (publishers) and apps (subscribers) are **completely decoupled** via the broker. The broker routes messages based on topic matching. This is the key scalability advantage of MQTT.

---

## 4.2 CoAP Request/Response Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1["COAP CLIENT MOBILE APP"]

    subgraph NET["CONSTRAINED NETWORK 6LOWPAN"]
        S1["COAP SERVER SMART METER"]
        S2["COAP SERVER LIGHT BULB"]
        S3["COAP SERVER TEMP SENSOR"]
    end

    C1 -- "CON GET TO SENSOR 1" --> S1
    S1 -- "ACK WITH JSON PAYLOAD" --> C1
    C1 -- "CON POST TO LIGHT 2" --> S2
    S2 -- "ACK 201 CREATED" --> C1
    C1 -- "NON GET TO TEMP 3" --> S3
    S3 -- "NON WITH DATA NO ACK" --> C3["SENSOR 3 RETURNS NON CONFIRMABLE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** CoAP uses **CON (Confirmable)** for critical operations and **NON (Non-confirmable)** for repeated telemetry (saves power by skipping ACK). This is a direct response to the limitations of TCP for constrained nodes.

---

```mermaid
graph LR

    WEB["WEB DASHBOARD CLIENT"]

    GW -- "HTTPS PUT STATE" --> LB

    API1 --> AUTH
```

> [!NOTE]

## 4.4 Hybrid IoT Protocol Stack (Most Common Production Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FIELD["FIELD NETWORK CONSTRAINED"]
        FS1["SOIL SENSOR 1"]
        FS2["SOIL SENSOR 2"]
        FS3["WEATHER STATION"]
    end

    GW["EDGE GATEWAY RASPBERRY PI"]

    subgraph CLOUDL["CLOUD PLATFORM"]
        MQB["MQTT BROKER EMQX"]

        MOB["MOBILE APP"]
        ERP["ERP SYSTEM SAP"]
    end

    FS1 -- "COAP UDP PORT 5683" --> GW
    FS2 -- "COAP UDP PORT 5683" --> GW
    FS3 -- "COAP UDP PORT 5683" --> GW

    GW -- "MQTT TCP PORT 8883 TLS" --> MQB
    MQB -- "INTERNAL PUB SUB" --> REST

    DASH -- "HTTPS GET" --> REST
    MOB -- "HTTPS GET POST" --> REST
    ERP -- "HTTPS ODATA" --> REST
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Production Reality:** Almost no real-world IoT deployment uses a single protocol. The standard pattern is **CoAP on the constrained field network → MQTT from gateway to cloud → REST API for application clients.** This is the architecture most likely to appear in a 14-mark KTU question.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are short, direct, and require 3-4 sentence answers with examples. Marks are awarded for **keyword coverage**.

---

### Question 1: Define MQTT. List any four typical use cases where MQTT is the preferred IoT protocol.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight **publish/subscribe messaging protocol** designed on top of TCP/IP for constrained devices and unreliable networks, standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922** and operating on **port 1883** (plain) or **8883** (TLS).

**Four typical use cases:**
1. **Smart Home Automation** — Telemetry from temperature, motion, and energy sensors to a central hub broker.
2. **Industrial IoT (IIoT) Monitoring** — Machine health data (vibration, pressure) from factory floors to SCADA systems.
3. **Connected Vehicle Fleet Telemetry** — GPS location, fuel level, and engine diagnostics from trucks over cellular networks.
4. **Healthcare Wearables** — Real-time heart rate and SpO2 streaming from patient wearables to hospital dashboards.

> **Marking Scheme (1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for the four use cases).**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between CoAP and REST API in terms of transport layer, header size, and reliability mechanism.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **CoAP** | **REST API** |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport Layer** | UDP (port 5683/5684) | TCP (port 80/443) |
| **Header Size** | 4 bytes (fixed) | Hundreds of bytes (variable, ~200 bytes minimum) |
| **Reliability Mechanism** | CON/ACK retransmission at application layer (over UDP) | TCP guarantees + HTTP status codes (200, 404, 500) |

> **Marking Scheme (1 mark per correct row × 3 rows = 3 marks).**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions have **internal choice**. Students must answer EITHER Option A OR Option B. Each option has two sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Publish/Subscribe architecture of MQTT** with a neat diagram. Discuss the three **Quality of Service (QoS) levels** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Identify and justify the **most suitable IoT communication protocol** for each of the following scenarios:
   1. A battery-powered soil moisture sensor in a remote farm transmitting every 15 minutes over LoRaWAN.
   2. A consumer smart bulb that must respond instantly to a smartphone toggle.
   3. A web dashboard fetching the last 24 hours of temperature data from a cloud IoT platform. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — MQTT Pub/Sub Architecture and QoS (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition and Architecture (3 marks)**

MQTT is a **publish/subscribe messaging protocol** standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922**, operating over TCP. It involves three main components:
- **Publisher** — Generates data (e.g., sensor).
- **Subscriber** — Consumes data (e.g., mobile app).
- **Broker** — Central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) that receives all messages from publishers and routes them to interested subscribers based on **topics**.

**Architecture Flow:**
1. The publisher sends a message with a **topic** (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`) to the broker.
2. The broker maintains a list of subscriptions and matches the topic.
3. The broker delivers the message to all matching subscribers.
4. Publishers and subscribers are **completely decoupled** — they do not know each other's IP address or existence.

**Step 2: QoS Levels (4 marks = 1.33 marks each)**

- **QoS 0 — At Most Once:** Fire-and-forget. Publisher sends the message once. No acknowledgement. Suitable for non-critical telemetry where occasional loss is acceptable (e.g., periodic temperature readings).
- **QoS 1 — At Least Once:** Publisher sends the message and waits for a **PUBACK** acknowledgement. If no ACK is received within a timeout, the message is retransmitted. May result in duplicate messages. Suitable for most IoT scenarios.
- **QoS 2 — Exactly Once:** Four-way handshake — **PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP**. Guarantees the message is delivered exactly once. Highest overhead, used for mission-critical commands (e.g., fire alarm, valve shut-off).

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["SENSOR 1 PUBLISHER"]
    P2["SENSOR 2 PUBLISHER"]
    B["MQTT BROKER"]
    S1["MOBILE APP SUBSCRIBER"]
    S2["DATABASE SUBSCRIBER"]

    P1 -- "TOPIC HOME TEMP" --> B
    P2 -- "TOPIC HOME MOTION" --> B
    B -- "MATCH TOPIC" --> S1
    B -- "MATCH TOPIC" --> S2
```

---

#### Part (b) — Protocol Selection for Three Scenarios (7 marks)

**Scenario 1: Battery-powered soil moisture sensor over LoRaWAN (2.33 marks)**

**Selected Protocol: CoAP**

**Justification:**
- LoRaWAN imposes strict **duty cycle limits** and **low data rate** (~0.3–50 kbps). CoAP's **4-byte header** minimizes overhead.
- CoAP runs over **UDP**, avoiding TCP's connection state which is unsuitable for sleepy battery nodes.
- 6LoWPAN/IPv6 stack commonly used in LoRaWAN gateways integrates seamlessly with CoAP.
- CBOR encoding further reduces payload size compared to JSON.

**Scenario 2: Consumer smart bulb responding to smartphone toggle (2.33 marks)**

**Selected Protocol: MQTT**

**Justification:**
- The bulb and phone are mains-powered and on stable Wi-Fi, so TCP overhead is acceptable.
- The user expects **instant push** when toggling — MQTT's native Pub/Sub delivers this without polling.
- The home automation platform (e.g., Home Assistant, AWS IoT) can scale to thousands of devices using **topic-based routing**.
- **QoS 1 (at-least-once)** ensures the toggle command is delivered even if the bulb temporarily disconnects.

**Scenario 3: Web dashboard fetching last 24 hours of temperature data (2.34 marks)**

**Selected Protocol: REST API**

**Justification:**
- The dashboard is a **web application** running in a browser — HTTP/HTTPS is the natural fit.
- A **GET request** to `/api/devices/{id}/telemetry?range=24h` is a standard REST call.
- The cloud platform (AWS, Azure) exposes REST APIs natively, and they pass through enterprise firewalls on port 443.
- **Caching headers** (ETag, Cache-Control) reduce redundant calls and improve performance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **operational architecture of CoAP** including its message types (CON, NON, ACK, RST), the **Observe pattern**, and **block-wise transfer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart city project deploys **10,000 LED streetlights** with integrated luminosity and motion sensors over a 6LoWPAN mesh network. The city wants to: (i) dim all streetlights in a zone simultaneously, (ii) detect individual light failures, and (iii) expose data to a public dashboard. Design and justify a **complete IoT protocol architecture** for this scenario. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — CoAP Operational Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition and Core Concept (2 marks)**

CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) is an **IETF-standardized (RFC 7252)** web transfer protocol designed for **constrained nodes and constrained networks**. It mimics HTTP semantics (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) but runs over **UDP** to minimize overhead. Default port is **5683** (plain) and **5684** (DTLS).

**Step 2: Message Types (2 marks = 0.5 mark each)**

- **CON (Confirmable):** The sender expects an ACK. Used for critical operations where reliability is needed. Triggers retransmission with exponential backoff if ACK is not received.
- **NON (Non-confirmable):** Fire-and-forget. No ACK expected. Used for repeated sensor readings where occasional loss is acceptable. Saves power.
- **ACK (Acknowledgement):** Confirms receipt of a CON message. Can carry a piggybacked response.
- **RST (Reset):** Sent when a peer cannot process a message (e.g., context lost after reboot).

**Step 3: Observe Pattern (1.5 marks)**

Defined in **RFC 7641**, the Observe pattern allows a CoAP client to **register interest in a resource state** on the server. The server then **pushes new state values** to the client whenever the resource changes. This effectively adds a **lightweight Pub/Sub capability** to CoAP. The relationship is identified by an Observe option in the request.

**Step 4: Block-Wise Transfer (1.5 marks)**

Defined in **RFC 7959**, this mechanism splits large payloads (e.g., firmware images) into **smaller blocks** that fit within a single 6LoWPAN fragment. Each block is sent as a separate CoAP message with `Block1` (request) or `Block2` (response) options. The receiver reassembles them. This is essential for **OTA firmware updates** on constrained devices.

---

#### Part (b) — Smart City Streetlight Architecture Design (7 marks)

**Step 1: Network Analysis (2 marks)**

- **Device class:** Class 1/2 (constrained 6LoWPAN nodes, often 8/32-bit MCUs).
- **Network type:** IEEE 802.15.4g / 6LoWPAN mesh, lossy, low bandwidth.
- **Scale:** 10,000 lights.
- **Requirements:**
  - (i) **Group dim command** (multicast).
  - (ii) **Failure detection** (per-device telemetry).
  - (iii) **Public dashboard** (web-friendly).

**Step 2: Protocol Selection Per Layer (4 marks)**

| **Layer** | **Selected Protocol** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Light-to-Border Router (Field) | **CoAP** | UDP multicast for group dim commands; Observe pattern for failure detection; 4-byte header fits 6LoWPAN. |
| Border Router to Cloud | **MQTT** | Pub/Sub scales to 10,000+ topics; QoS 1 for failure alerts; broker buffers messages during network outages. |
| Cloud to Public Dashboard | **REST API** | Web/mobile friendly, HTTPS on port 443, JSON payloads, easy third-party integration. |

**Step 3: Complete Architecture Diagram (1 mark)**

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FIELD["6LOWPAN MESH FIELD LAYER"]
        L1["STREETLIGHT ZONE A 1"]
        L2["STREETLIGHT ZONE A 2"]
        L3["STREETLIGHT ZONE B 1"]
    end

    BR["BORDER ROUTER EDGE GATEWAY"]

    subgraph CLOUDL["CLOUD LAYER"]
        MQB["MQTT BROKER"]
        RESTS["REST API SERVER"]
        DASH["PUBLIC WEB DASHBOARD"]
    end

    L1 -- "COAP MULTICAST DIM" --> L2
    L1 -- "COAP CON OBSERVE STATUS" --> BR
    L2 -- "COAP CON OBSERVE STATUS" --> BR
    L3 -- "COAP CON OBSERVE STATUS" --> BR

    BR -- "MQTT PUBLISH TELEMETRY" --> MQB
    MQB -- "INTERNAL HANDLER" --> RESTS
    DASH -- "HTTPS GET LIGHTS" --> RESTS
```

**Justification Summary:**
- **CoAP multicast** for group dimming — one command, thousands of lights, low overhead.
- **CoAP Observe** for real-time failure detection — each light pushes its status when state changes.
- **MQTT** to forward telemetry from border router to cloud — handles intermittent connectivity.
- **REST API** for the public dashboard — developer-friendly, standard HTTP, easy authentication with OAuth 2.0.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the transport layer:** Students often write "MQTT uses UDP" or "CoAP uses TCP" — this is **factually wrong** and costs full marks. **MQTT = TCP, CoAP = UDP, REST = TCP.**
> 2. **Writing "MQTT is for HTTP" or "REST is Pub/Sub":** These are **architectural style mismatches**. MQTT is Pub/Sub; REST is Request/Response. Mixing them shows fundamental misunderstanding.
> 3. **Forgetting the port numbers:** Examiners often award 1 mark just for the correct default port (1883 for MQTT, 5683 for CoAP, 443 for HTTPS).
> 4. **No diagram in 7-mark questions:** Always include a **Mermaid/block diagram**. A missing diagram costs **at least 1–2 marks** in 7+7 questions.
> 5. **Choosing a single protocol for a hybrid scenario:** When a question has field sensors + cloud + dashboard, **never** pick just one protocol. Always propose a **hybrid stack** (CoAP + MQTT + REST).
> 6. **Skipping QoS discussion in MQTT answers:** If the question mentions MQTT, the examiner expects **QoS 0/1/2** to be explained. Omitting it costs 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Vague justifications:** "MQTT is lightweight" is too vague. Say: *"MQTT has a 2-byte fixed header, ~5x lower overhead than HTTP, and supports QoS for guaranteed delivery."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read before every exam):**

- ✅ **MQTT** is a **Pub/Sub** protocol over **TCP** (port 1883/8883), with a **2-byte fixed header** and **3 QoS levels** (0, 1, 2). Best for **real-time push, large fleets, unreliable networks**.
- ✅ **CoAP** is a **Request/Response** protocol over **UDP** (port 5683/5684), with a **4-byte fixed header**, **CON/NON/ACK/RST** message types, **Observe** for push, and **Block-wise transfer** for large payloads. Best for **constrained Class 1 devices, multicast, lossy networks**.
- ✅ **REST API** uses **HTTP/HTTPS** (port 80/443), with methods **GET/POST/PUT/DELETE**, stateless architecture, **JSON/XML** payloads. Best for **cloud-to-web/mobile, enterprise integration, dashboards**.
- ✅ **Typical MQTT use cases:** Smart home, IIoT, vehicle telemetry, healthcare wearables, smart metering, logistics.
- ✅ **Typical CoAP use cases:** Smart lighting, smart meters, building automation, precision agriculture, in-body medical patches, industrial WSN.
- ✅ **Typical REST API use cases:** Cloud dashboards, mobile apps, OTA updates, ERP integration, device shadow sync, public Smart City APIs.
- ✅ **Hybrid architectures are the norm:** CoAP (field) → MQTT (gateway-to-cloud) → REST (app-to-cloud).
- ✅ **Decision criteria:** Device class, bandwidth, latency, reliability, multicast need, firewall, and ecosystem maturity.
- ✅ **Standard bodies:** MQTT → OASIS/ISO; CoAP → IETF (RFC 7252); REST → IETF (Roy Fielding's dissertation).
- ✅ **Remember the ports:** 1883/8883 (MQTT), 5683/5684 (CoAP), 80/443 (REST). Examiners love to test these.
- ✅ **Overhead rule of thumb:** REST ≫ MQTT > CoAP. CoAP is ~17x lighter than REST in 6LoWPAN; MQTT is ~5x lighter.
- ✅ **Security:** MQTT → TLS; CoAP → DTLS; REST → TLS (HTTPS).
- ✅ **Pub/Sub is MQTT's superpower** — native push without polling. CoAP gets Pub/Sub via the **Observe** pattern. REST needs **WebSocket** for push.
- ✅ **Always end with a diagram** in 7+7 questions — it is the single biggest mark-booster for protocol questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing - Overview of Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET785 - INTERNET OF THINGS BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing - Overview of Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (NIST Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (NIST SP 800-145):** *Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.*

The five **Essential Characteristics** as per NIST are:

1. **On-demand self-service**
2. **Broad network access**
3. **Resource pooling**
4. **Rapid elasticity**
5. **Measured service**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Electricity Grid

> [!NOTE]
> Think of cloud computing exactly like the **electricity grid**. You do not buy a personal power plant for your house; you simply plug into a socket and pay only for the units (kWh) you consume. If you need more power (say, to run an industrial machine), you scale up instantly. If you need less, you scale down. The grid provider manages the generators, transformers, and distribution — you just consume. **Cloud computing delivers compute, storage, and networking "as a utility."**

For an **IoT system**, this analogy is perfect:
- The **IoT devices** = the appliances in your house.
- The **Cloud** = the electricity grid and power plant.
- The **sensors stream** = the current drawn.
- The **billing/metering** = metered service (pay-per-use).

## 1.3 Core Architectural Vision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Architecture** refers to the **structural components and sub-components** required to deliver a cloud computing service. It is typically divided into two halves:
> - **Frontend** (Client Side) — what the user/device sees.
> - **Backend** (Service Provider Side) — the infrastructure delivering the service.

A third mediating layer, the **Network (Internet)**, glues them together over standard protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, WebSockets).

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

The following are the **standard deployment and service metrics** that appear in KTU 2024 scheme question papers:

| Metric | Typical Value / Notation |
|---|---|
| Availability Tier | **99.9% ("three nines")** to **99.999% ("five nines")** |
| Elasticity Window | Seconds to minutes |
| Multi-tenancy | Single instance serving multiple tenants |
| Service Granularity | IaaS < PaaS < SaaS (in abstraction) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Cloud Architecture (The Reference Stack)

The **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (NCCRA)** divides the cloud into the following logical roles:

- **Cloud Consumer** — the person/organization that acquires and uses cloud services (e.g., an IoT dashboard user).
- **Cloud Provider** — the entity that makes the service available (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP).
- **Cloud Auditor** — an independent party that evaluates service conformance.
- **Cloud Broker** — an intermediary that manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services, and negotiates relationships between providers and consumers.
- **Cloud Carrier** — the intermediary that provides connectivity and transport of cloud services (e.g., ISPs, telecom operators).

## 2.2 Frontend vs. Backend — Component Breakdown

**Frontend Components** (visible to the IoT device / user):
- Thin client / Thick client / Mobile app / Web browser / IoT gateway
- Network interface (LAN/Wi-Fi/4G/5G/Satellite)
- Local cache & authentication module

**Backend Components** (managed by the cloud provider):
- **Application Layer** — SaaS apps, APIs, dashboards
- **Platform Layer** — OS, middleware, runtimes (PaaS)
- **Infrastructure Layer** — servers, storage, network (IaaS)
- **Virtualization Layer** — hypervisor, containers (Docker/Kubernetes)
- **Physical Layer** — actual data center hardware (racks, power, cooling)

## 2.3 Service Models — The SPI Acronym

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to **compare and contrast** the three service models. Memorize this acronym: **SPI = SaaS, PaaS, IaaS**.

| Service Model | Full Form | User Manages | Provider Manages | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Apps, Data | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine, DigitalOcean |
| **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | Apps, Data | OS, Middleware, Runtime, plus all IaaS layers | Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku |
| **SaaS** | Software-as-a-Service | Only user data and access configuration | Everything else | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Dropbox |

> [!NOTE]
> **In IoT context:** A sensor manufacturer may use **IaaS** to host its raw time-series data, **PaaS** to develop its analytics engine (e.g., using AWS IoT Core), and the end-user accesses dashboards via **SaaS** (e.g., a mobile monitoring app).

## 2.4 Deployment Models

| Deployment Model | Definition | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | Open to general public; owned by provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) | IoT startups, web apps |
| **Private Cloud** | Operated solely for one organization | Banks, defense, hospitals |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Composition of two or more clouds (public + private) bound by standardization | IoT with sensitive edge + scalable cloud |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., govt., healthcare) | Smart city consortiums |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While cloud architecture is not a numerical subject, the following **quantitative relationships** are tested in KTU exams as 3-mark and 14-mark analytical questions.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Service Availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless (ratio) | SLA evaluation |
| Annual Downtime | $D = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | minutes/year | SLA penalty calculation |
| Pay-per-Use Cost | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (r_i \times t_i \times u_i)$ | currency (INR/USD) | Billing model analysis |
| Storage Provisioning | $S_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} V_{j} \times R_{j}$ | GB or TB | Capacity planning |
| Elasticity Ratio | $E = \frac{\text{Peak Load}}{\text{Average Load}}$ | dimensionless | Auto-scaling design |

Where:
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair
- $r_i$ = hourly rate of resource $i$
- $t_i$ = duration of use of resource $i$
- $u_i$ = utilization factor of resource $i$
- $V_j$ = volume of virtual disk $j$
- $R_j$ = replication factor (1, 2, 3 for N+1 redundancy)
- $525{,}600$ = total minutes in a non-leap year

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **IoT + Cloud** ecosystem, cloud architecture is the **central nervous system** that:
- Ingests **billions of sensor readings** per day via message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS IoT Core).
- Provides **unlimited horizontal scalability** for analytics and ML inference.
- Enables **geographic redundancy** through multi-region deployment.
- Supports **event-driven serverless functions** (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions) reacting to sensor triggers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> Cloud architecture is a conceptual/architectural subject, so the "derivations" below are **analytical breakdowns** that KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers (with sub-part derivations, case analyses, and parametric comparisons).

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Service Availability and Downtime

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 14-mark sub-part):**
*An IoT cloud service advertises a monthly availability of $99.95\%$. Calculate (i) the allowable monthly downtime in minutes, (ii) the annual downtime in hours, and (iii) comment on its suitability for a healthcare monitoring IoT application.*

### Step 1 — State the Given

$$A_{monthly} = 99.95\% = 0.9995$$

Monthly time base = **30 days × 24 h × 60 min = 43,200 minutes**.

### Step 2 — Compute the Monthly Downtime Fraction

$$D_{fraction} = 1 - A_{monthly} = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Absolute Downtime

$$D_{monthly} = D_{fraction} \times 43{,}200$$

$$D_{monthly} = 0.0005 \times 43{,}200 = 21.6 \text{ minutes}$$

### Step 4 — Annualize the Downtime

$$D_{annual} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$$

$$D_{annual} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8 \text{ minutes}$$

Converting to hours:

$$D_{annual, hours} = \frac{262.8}{60} = 4.38 \text{ hours/year}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **healthcare IoT** (e.g., remote patient ECG monitoring), 4.38 hours of yearly downtime is **unacceptable** because life-critical data may be lost. The required SLA for such use cases is **99.999% ("five nines")**, giving:
> $$D_{annual} = 0.00001 \times 525{,}600 \approx 5.26 \text{ minutes/year}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SPI Service Models

The KTU 14-mark answer often asks: *"Differentiate IaaS, PaaS and SaaS with neat diagrams."* The full analytical solution requires a **3-axis comparison**:

**Axis 1 — Abstraction Level (low to high):**
$$\text{IaaS} < \text{PaaS} < \text{SaaS}$$

**Axis 2 — User Responsibility Surface Area:**
$$R_{user, IaaS} > R_{user, PaaS} > R_{user, SaaS}$$

**Axis 3 — Time-to-Market:**
$$T_{deploy, IaaS} > T_{deploy, PaaS} > T_{deploy, SaaS}$$

**Verbal Justification:**
- IaaS gives raw VMs → user installs everything → slowest deployment.
- PaaS gives runtime + OS → user only deploys code → fast.
- SaaS gives ready software → user only configures → instant.

## 3.3 Cost Modeling Derivation (Pay-per-Use)

A typical KTU analytical question:
*A startup uses 4 virtual machines at ₹4/hr each, for 18 hours/day, 26 days/month, with 70% utilization. Compute the monthly bill.*

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula

$$C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (r_i \times t_i \times u_i)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$n = 4, \quad r = 4 \text{ INR/hr}, \quad t = 18 \times 26 = 468 \text{ hr}, \quad u = 0.70$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$C_{total} = 4 \times 4 \times 468 \times 0.70$$

$$C_{total} = 4{,}390.4 \text{ INR/month}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> This is why cloud computing is attractive to IoT startups — **opex replaces capex**. The same workload on dedicated hardware would require ~₹2–3 lakh upfront capex for servers, plus power, cooling, and a sysadmin.

## 3.4 Analytical Hierarchy of Deployment Models

For KTU 14-mark questions asking *"Which deployment model is suitable for X scenario?"*, the analytical decision tree is:

$$\text{Is the data highly sensitive?} \Rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Private Cloud}$$
$$\text{No} \Rightarrow \text{Is cost the primary concern?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Public Cloud}$$
$$\text{No} \Rightarrow \text{Are there bursty workloads?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Hybrid Cloud}$$
$$\text{No} \Rightarrow \text{Shared industry goals?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Community Cloud}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Cloud Computing Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["IoT Devices and Sensors"]:::iot
    node2["Cloud Consumer Frontend - Web Mobile Gateway"]:::frontend
    node3["Internet and Network Carrier Layer"]:::net
    node4["Cloud Provider Backend"]:::backend
    node5["Application Layer - SaaS APIs Dashboards"]:::saas
    node6["Platform Layer - PaaS Runtimes Middleware"]:::paas
    node7["Infrastructure Layer - IaaS VMs Storage Network"]:::iaas
    node8["Virtualization and Container Layer"]:::virt
    node9["Physical Data Center Hardware"]:::phy
    node10["Cloud Auditor Independent Verification"]:::audit
    node11["Cloud Broker Service Intermediary"]:::broker
    node12["Cloud Carrier Network Provider"]:::carrier

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node3 -.-> node12
    node4 -.-> node10
    node4 -.-> node11

    classDef iot fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef frontend fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#1a237e,color:#000
    classDef backend fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef saas fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef paas fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef iaas fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
    classDef virt fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef phy fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,color:#000
    classDef audit fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef broker fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    classDef carrier fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
```

## 4.2 SPI Service Model Stack Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph userctrl["User Manages"]
        udata["User Data and Access Configuration"]
    end

    subgraph saas["SaaS - Software as a Service"]
        sApp["Applications"]
        sData["Data"]
        sOS["OS Runtime Middleware"]
        sVirt["Virtualization"]
        sPhys["Servers Storage Network"]
    end

    subgraph paas["PaaS - Platform as a Service"]
        pApp["Applications"]
        pData["Data"]
        pOS["OS Runtime Middleware"]
        pVirt["Virtualization"]
        pPhys["Servers Storage Network"]
    end

    subgraph iaas["IaaS - Infrastructure as a Service"]
        iApp["Applications"]
        iData["Data"]
        iOS["OS Runtime Middleware"]
        iVirt["Virtualization"]
        iPhys["Servers Storage Network"]
    end

    udata --> sApp
    udata --> pApp
    udata --> iApp
    iPhys --> pPhys
    pPhys --> sPhys
```

## 4.3 Deployment Model Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Choose Cloud Deployment Model"]
    q1{"Is data highly sensitive or regulated?"}
    q2{"Is cost minimization the top priority?"}
    q3{"Do workloads have variable bursty demand?"}
    q4{"Do multiple orgs share common goals?"}

    aPri["Private Cloud"]
    aPub["Public Cloud"]
    aHyb["Hybrid Cloud"]
    aCom["Community Cloud"]

    startA --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> aPri
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> aPub
    q2 -- No --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> aHyb
    q3 -- No --> q4
    q4 -- Yes --> aCom
    q4 -- No --> aPub
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. Define Cloud Computing. List the five essential characteristics as per NIST.**

**Model Answer:**

Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort.

The five essential characteristics are:
1. **On-demand self-service**
2. **Broad network access**
3. **Resource pooling**
4. **Rapid elasticity**
5. **Measured service**

> [!NOTE]
> *Valuation Tip:* Writing only 3 of 5 characteristics will cap your marks at 2/3. Always list **all five**.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS and SaaS in one line each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Provides virtualized computing resources like VMs, storage, and networks over the internet. *Example: AWS EC2.*
- **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Provides a runtime platform and tools to develop, test, and deploy applications without managing the underlying infrastructure. *Example: Google App Engine.*
- **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** Delivers ready-to-use software applications over the internet on a subscription basis. *Example: Gmail.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 Style]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Cloud Computing Reference Architecture** as defined by NIST. Describe the roles of **Cloud Consumer, Provider, Auditor, Broker, and Carrier**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **three service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)** with a comparative table. Mention one real-world example and one IoT use case for each. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (NCCRA)** identifies five major actors:

1. **Cloud Consumer** – Uses the services. Example: A startup hosting its IoT dashboard on AWS.
2. **Cloud Provider** – Maintains the services. Example: AWS, Azure, Google Cloud.
3. **Cloud Auditor** – Independent third party that evaluates security, privacy, and performance.
4. **Cloud Broker** – Manages cloud use, performance, and delivery; negotiates between consumer and provider.
5. **Cloud Carrier** – Provides the network connectivity (ISPs, telecom operators).

The architecture has two halves: **Frontend (consumer side)** and **Backend (provider side)** connected through a **Network (carrier)**. See the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual reference.

**Conclusion:** The NCCRA framework decouples service usage from infrastructure ownership, enabling trust, interoperability, and portability in IoT-cloud deployments.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | Platform-as-a-Service | Software-as-a-Service |
| Abstraction Level | Low | Medium | High |
| User Manages | OS, Runtime, Apps, Data | Apps and Data | Only Data and Configuration |
| Provider Manages | Servers, Storage, Network, Virtualization | + OS, Middleware, Runtime | + Everything |
| Time-to-Market | Slow | Medium | Instant |
| Real-world Example | AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine | Heroku, Google App Engine | Office 365, Salesforce |
| IoT Use Case | Hosting raw sensor data lake | Building analytics engine on AWS IoT Core | End-user mobile health-monitoring app |

**Conclusion:** The choice between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS is driven by the level of control desired, the speed of deployment needed, and the technical expertise of the IoT team.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **four deployment models** of cloud computing. For each, give one real-world scenario where it is most suitable. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An IoT startup provisions **6 virtual machines** on AWS at **₹5/hr per VM**, running **20 hours/day for 28 days/month** at **75% utilization**. Calculate the **monthly bill**. If the same workload were migrated to a **public cloud with 99.99% availability**, calculate the **maximum allowable annual downtime in minutes** and comment on whether this SLA is suitable for a **remote cardiac monitoring system**. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. **Public Cloud** — Open to general public. Owned by third-party provider. Best for: IoT startups, social media apps. *Example: AWS.*
2. **Private Cloud** — Used exclusively by one organization. Best for: Banking, defense, hospitals with strict compliance. *Example: On-premise OpenStack.*
3. **Hybrid Cloud** — Combines public and private. Best for: Enterprises with sensitive data on-premise but bursty workloads in public. *Example: IoT gateway on private + analytics on AWS.*
4. **Community Cloud** — Shared by organizations with common concerns. Best for: Smart city consortium, government health network.

**Conclusion:** Deployment model selection is governed by data sensitivity, cost, scalability, and regulatory requirements.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Monthly Bill:**

$$C_{total} = n \times r \times t \times u$$

$$C_{total} = 6 \times 5 \times (20 \times 28) \times 0.75$$

$$C_{total} = 6 \times 5 \times 560 \times 0.75$$

$$C_{total} = 12{,}600 \text{ INR/month}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Downtime:**

$$D_{annual} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 525{,}600$$

$$D_{annual} = 0.0001 \times 525{,}600 = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

**Step 3 — Suitability for Remote Cardiac Monitoring:**

52.56 minutes/year downtime equals roughly **once every 7 days, 7.5 minutes of outage**. For a **cardiac patient**, a 7-minute gap in ECG data transmission could mean missing a **critical arrhythmia event**. Therefore, the **99.99% ("four nines") SLA is inadequate**. The recommended SLA is **99.999% ("five nines")**, which provides only ~5.26 minutes/year of downtime.

**Conclusion:** The monthly bill is ₹12,600, and the SLA must be upgraded to "five nines" for life-critical IoT applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - **Forgetting units:** Always write `INR/month`, `minutes/year`, etc. Numeric answers without units lose 0.5–1 mark.
> - **Wrong time base:** Students often use `365 days × 24 h × 60 min = 525{,}600` correctly but forget to convert seconds.
> - **Misspelling NIST acronym:** Spelling it as "NISTA" or "NISC" is an instant red flag for the examiner.
> - **Omitting 5th essential characteristic:** Memorize all 5 NIST characteristics.
> - **Confusing PaaS and SaaS examples:** Heroku is **PaaS**, not SaaS. Gmail is **SaaS**, not PaaS.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cloud Computing Overview of Architecture**

- ⭐ **NIST definition** is the gold-standard definition; always quote it verbatim in exams.
- ⭐ **5 Essential Characteristics:** On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service.
- ⭐ **SPI Acronym** = SaaS, PaaS, IaaS (in increasing abstraction).
- ⭐ **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- ⭐ **5 Actors in NCCRA:** Consumer, Provider, Auditor, Broker, Carrier.
- ⭐ **Key Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$; $D_{annual} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes.
- ⭐ **Pay-per-use formula:** $C_{total} = \sum r_i \times t_i \times u_i$.
- ⭐ **IoT-Cloud synergy:** Sensors → Gateway → Cloud (ingest, store, analyze, visualize).
- ⭐ **Healthcare-grade SLA:** 99.999% (five nines) = ~5.26 min/year downtime.
- ⭐ **Always draw a labeled Mermaid/markdown diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners reward visual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Models - Software-as-a-Service (SaaS), Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS), and Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET785 - INTERNET OF THINGS BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Models - Software-as-a-Service (SaaS), Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS), and Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Cloud Service Models?

In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT) Based System Design**, the "Cloud" refers to a vast network of remote servers hosted on the internet that store, manage, process, and analyze data generated by IoT devices, rather than relying on local servers or personal computers. A **Cloud Service Model** defines the category of services (compute, storage, networking, software, runtime) that a cloud provider offers to its consumers and, more critically, defines the boundary of responsibility between the **Cloud Provider** and the **Consumer/User**.

The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** officially categorizes cloud computing into three primary service models, which form the backbone of Module 4 in the PEAET785 syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAET785 - Module 4)**
> Students must be able to **distinguish clearly between SaaS, PaaS, and IaaS** with respect to:
> - What the **user manages** vs. what the **provider manages**
> - Suitable **IoT use-cases** for each model
> - **Examples** of real-world platforms (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud)

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Cloud Service Models

| Service Model | Full Form | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | Rent raw hardware (servers, storage, network) |
| **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | Rent a ready-made development & deployment platform |
| **SaaS** | Software-as-a-Service** | Use ready-to-run software over the internet |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Pizza-as-a-Service" Model 🍕

This is the **most widely used pedagogical analogy** in cloud computing and is highly recommended for KTU viva voce preparation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Pizza-as-a-Service)**
>
> Imagine you are hosting a party and need pizza. You have four options:
>
> 1. **Make it from scratch (On-Premise):** You buy flour, cheese, oven, make dough, bake, serve. You manage **everything**.
>
> 2. **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** You go to a kitchen rental place. They give you a pre-heated **oven, gas, and a counter (Infrastructure)**. You still bring the **dough, cheese, and toppings (Platform & Software)** and cook it yourself.
>
> 3. **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** You call a place that gives you a **pre-made pizza base, sauce, and oven (Platform)**. You only supply the **toppings and bake it (your Application & Data)**.
>
> 4. **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** You simply **order a finished pizza delivered to your door**. You don't know or care how it was made. You just **eat (use) the software**.

---

## 1.4 Why Cloud Models Matter in IoT Systems

In an IoT ecosystem, thousands of sensors generate **terabytes of data** every day. Sending this to a local server is impractical. Cloud models provide:

- **Scalability** → Instantly scale storage/compute as device count grows
- **Elasticity** → Pay only for what you use (utility-based pricing)
- **High Availability** → **99.9% uptime SLA** (Service Level Agreement)
- **Remote Accessibility** → Monitor your IoT fleet from anywhere
- **Cost Efficiency** → No upfront CapEx (Capital Expenditure); convert to **OpEx (Operational Expenditure)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board-Approved)**
> **Cloud Computing (NIST SP 800-145):** A model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with **minimal management effort** or service provider interaction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)

### 2.1.1 Definition
**IaaS** provides fundamental computing resources — **servers, virtual machines (VMs), storage, networks, and load balancers** — over the internet on a **pay-as-you-go** basis. The user has the **highest level of control** and is responsible for installing the Operating System (OS), middleware, runtime, applications, and data.

### 2.1.2 Characteristics
- **Provisioning time:** Minutes (vs. weeks for physical servers)
- **Resource Pooling:** Multi-tenant architecture
- **Elasticity:** Vertical and horizontal scaling
- **Metered Billing:** Pay per hour/second of VM usage
- **Control:** User controls OS up to the application layer

### 2.1.3 Real-World Examples for IoT
- **Amazon Web Services (AWS) EC2** — Virtual servers for IoT data ingestion
- **Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines** — Hosting custom MQTT brokers
- **Google Compute Engine (GCE)**
- **DigitalOcean Droplets, Linode**

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Use-Case:** A smart-city project receives sensor data from 1 million streetlights. The team uses **AWS EC2** to host a custom Kafka cluster that ingests and processes telemetry.

---

## 2.2 Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)

### 2.2.1 Definition
**PaaS** delivers a **ready-to-use platform** — including OS, middleware, runtime, development tools, databases, and BI services — on which developers can **build, test, deploy, and manage IoT applications** without worrying about the underlying infrastructure.

### 2.2.2 Characteristics
- **Developer-Focused:** Built-in SDKs, APIs, and DevOps pipelines
- **No OS Management:** Provider handles OS, patching, security
- **Built-in Services:** Auto-scaling, load balancing, database management
- **Faster Time-to-Market:** Focus only on application code

### 2.2.3 Real-World Examples for IoT
- **Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Services** — Generic PaaS
- **IoT-Specific PaaS:** **AWS IoT Core, Microsoft Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT Platform, ThingSpeak, Blynk**
- **Heroku, Salesforce Lightning Platform**

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Use-Case:** A startup building a wearable health monitor uses **AWS IoT Core (PaaS)** to register devices, ingest MQTT messages, and trigger Lambda rules — without ever managing a server.

---

## 2.3 Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)

### 2.3.1 Definition
**SaaS** delivers **fully functional, ready-to-use applications** hosted on the cloud and accessed via a **web browser or thin client/API**. The user does not manage anything except their own data and user configuration. It is the **most user-friendly** model.

### 2.3.2 Characteristics
- **Zero Local Installation:** Runs entirely in browser
- **Subscription-Based:** Monthly/annual per-user pricing
- **Multi-Tenant:** Single instance serves many organizations
- **Automatic Updates:** Provider rolls out patches/features automatically
- **Accessible Anywhere:** Any device with a browser

### 2.3.3 Real-World Examples for IoT
- **General SaaS:** Gmail, Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Dropbox, Salesforce CRM, Slack, Zoom
- **IoT-Specific SaaS:** **ThingWorx (PTC), IBM Watson IoT Platform dashboards, Losant, Ubidots, Particle Cloud Console, AWS IoT SiteWise (dashboard layer)**

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Use-Case:** A factory manager logs into the **Ubidots dashboard (SaaS)** from a tablet to view real-time temperature graphs of all cold-storage rooms — no installation, no server.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet: Responsibility Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Shared Responsibility Model** is the **single most important concept** for KTU board exams on this topic. Memorize the table below.

| Stack Layer | On-Premise | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Application** | User | User | User | Provider |
| **Data** | User | User | User | User (limited) |
| **Runtime / Middleware** | User | User | Provider | Provider |
| **Operating System** | User | User | Provider | Provider |
| **Virtualization** | User | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Servers (Compute)** | User | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Storage** | User | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Networking** | User | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Physical Data Center** | User | Provider | Provider | Provider |

**Legend:** **Provider** = Cloud Vendor (AWS, Azure) | **User** = Consumer/Developer

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility: Why This Matters in IoT Projects

- **Device Count Drives Model Choice:** A 10-sensor home project may use **SaaS (Blynk)**; a 1M-sensor smart-city needs **IaaS (custom EC2 clusters)** with **PaaS (IoT Core)** on top.
- **Cost Equation (Total Cost of Ownership):**
  $$\text{TCO} = C_{\text{CapEx}} + C_{\text{OpEx}} + C_{\text{Maintenance}} + C_{\text{Downtime}}$$
  Cloud models reduce **CapEx** to near zero by replacing hardware purchases with monthly subscriptions.
- **Latency Consideration:** Pure cloud SaaS may add **20–100 ms latency**. For **real-time industrial IoT**, **edge computing** is preferred over centralized SaaS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Deep Comparison of the Three Models

This matrix is the **highest-yield content** for KTU 14-mark questions. Students should be able to write a clean 7-point comparison in the exam.

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | Platform-as-a-Service | Software-as-a-Service |
| **Audience** | System Admins, DevOps, Network Architects | Application Developers | End Users / Business Teams |
| **User Manages** | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Apps, Data | Apps and Data | Only their own data & user settings |
| **Provider Manages** | Hardware, Network, Storage, Virtualization | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Hardware | Everything except user data |
| **Flexibility** | **Highest** (full control) | Medium (constrained to platform) | **Lowest** (fixed features) |
| **Portability** | High (can move VMs) | Vendor lock-in risk | Vendor lock-in (high) |
| **Development Effort** | Highest | Lowest for app dev | No dev needed (just configure) |
| **Time-to-Deploy** | Hours to days | Minutes to hours | Immediate (login & use) |
| **Cost Model** | Pay per VM-hour, GB-storage | Pay per app instance, request | Pay per user/month (subscription) |
| **Scalability** | Manual or scripted auto-scaling | Automatic, built-in | Automatic, transparent to user |
| **Skill Required** | Linux, Networking, Virtualization | Programming, Platform APIs | Basic web/mobile usage |
| **IoT Examples** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs | AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Heroku | Ubidots, ThingSpeak, Salesforce IoT |
| **Analogy** | Renting an empty kitchen | Renting a kitchen with stove & oven ready | Ordering pizza delivery |
| **Security Model** | User secures OS & above | Provider secures platform; user secures app | Provider secures everything; user secures login/password |

---

## 3.2 Derived Trade-Off Equation: When to Choose What

The choice between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS can be derived from a **Control vs. Convenience trade-off**, which the KTU syllabus often tests via case-study questions.

$$\text{Control} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\text{Convenience}}$$

In words:
- **IaaS** = **Maximum Control, Minimum Convenience**
- **PaaS** = **Balanced Control and Convenience**
- **SaaS** = **Minimum Control, Maximum Convenience**

### 3.2.1 Decision Logic (Step-by-Step)

For any IoT project, apply this KTU-style 4-step selection logic:

**Step 1: Define the goal.**
- Goal = "Use a ready dashboard" → **SaaS**
- Goal = "Build a custom IoT pipeline" → Go to Step 2

**Step 2: Check developer resources.**
- No OS/sysadmin team → **PaaS** (e.g., AWS IoT Core)
- Strong DevOps team → Go to Step 3

**Step 3: Check customization needs.**
- Need full control over OS, network, custom protocols → **IaaS** (e.g., raw EC2)
- Standard protocols (MQTT/HTTP) are sufficient → **PaaS**

**Step 4: Validate with cost.**
- Run a sample cost calculator:
$$\text{Monthly Cost}_{\text{IaaS}} = (n_{\text{VM}} \times c_{\text{VM-hr}} \times 720) + (s_{\text{GB}} \times c_{\text{storage}})$$

$$\text{Monthly Cost}_{\text{PaaS}} = (n_{\text{requests}} \times c_{\text{req}}) + (d_{\text{DB-GB}} \times c_{\text{DB}})$$

$$\text{Monthly Cost}_{\text{SaaS}} = (n_{\text{users}} \times c_{\text{per-user}})$$

Where:
- $n_{\text{VM}}$ = Number of virtual machines
- $c_{\text{VM-hr}}$ = Cost per VM-hour (e.g., \$0.05)
- $s_{\text{GB}}$ = Storage in GB
- $c_{\text{storage}}$ = Per-GB storage cost
- $n_{\text{requests}}$ = API/IoT messages per month
- $c_{\text{req}}$ = Cost per million requests
- $n_{\text{users}}$ = Licensed users
- $c_{\text{per-user}}$ = Subscription per user/month

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For small-scale IoT (< 1,000 devices), **SaaS** is the most cost-effective. For hyperscale IoT (> 1M devices), **IaaS + custom PaaS** gives the lowest per-device cost despite higher management overhead.

---

## 3.3 The "Anything-as-a-Service" (XaaS) Continuum

Modern cloud models extend beyond the three core services. KTU may ask short notes on these:

$$\text{XaaS} = \{ \text{IaaS}, \text{PaaS}, \text{SaaS}, \text{FaaS}, \text{DaaS}, \text{BaaS}, \text{MaaS} \ldots \}$$

- **FaaS (Function-as-a-Service):** AWS Lambda, Azure Functions — serverless
- **DaaS (Data-as-a-Service):** Snowflake, BigQuery
- **BaaS (Backend-as-a-Service):** Firebase, AWS Amplify
- **MaaS (Monitoring-as-a-Service):** Datadog, New Relic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** While the syllabus focuses on the three primary models, students are expected to **briefly mention XaaS** in 14-mark questions to demonstrate broader awareness.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cloud Service Model Stack (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram shows the **layered responsibility model** — the most frequently requested diagram in KTU board exams for this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["SAAS LAYER - User just uses the software"]
        A1["Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Ubidots Dashboard"]
    end
    subgraph B["PAAS LAYER - User builds apps on a ready platform"]
        B1["Application - USER MANAGES"]
        B2["Data - USER MANAGES"]
        B3["Runtime - PROVIDER MANAGES"]
        B4["Middleware - PROVIDER MANAGES"]
        B5["Operating System - PROVIDER MANAGES"]
    end
    subgraph C["IAAS LAYER - User manages OS and above"]
        C1["Application - USER MANAGES"]
        C2["Data - USER MANAGES"]
        C3["Runtime - USER MANAGES"]
        C4["Middleware - USER MANAGES"]
        C5["Operating System - USER MANAGES"]
        C6["Virtualization - PROVIDER MANAGES"]
        C7["Servers - PROVIDER MANAGES"]
        C8["Storage - PROVIDER MANAGES"]
        C9["Networking - PROVIDER MANAGES"]
    end
    A --> B --> C
    A1 -.uses.-> B1
    B1 -.extends.-> C1
```

**Interpretation:** As you move **downward from SaaS to IaaS**, the user takes on **more management responsibility** and gains **more control**. The provider's responsibility shrinks as the user's grows.

---

## 4.2 IoT Cloud Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

This diagram shows **how the three service models co-exist in a real IoT system**, which is the type of architecture question KTU frequently asks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEV["IoT DEVICE LAYER"]
        D1["Sensor Node - Temperature"]
        D2["Actuator - Smart Valve"]
        GW["Edge Gateway - Raspberry Pi"]
    end
    subgraph PAAS["PAAS LAYER - AWS IoT Core"]
        P1["Device Registry"]
        P2["Message Broker - MQTT"]
        P3["Rules Engine"]
    end
    subgraph SAAS["SAAS LAYER"]
        S1["Ubidots Dashboard"]
        S2["Mobile App Alerts"]
        S3["Slack Notifications"]
    end
    subgraph IAAS["IAAS LAYER"]
        I1["EC2 VM - Custom ML Model"]
        I2["S3 Bucket - Raw Data Lake"]
        I3["PostgreSQL - Time Series DB"]
    end
    D1 --> GW
    D2 --> GW
    GW -->|"MQTT/TLS"| P2
    P2 --> P1
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> S1
    P3 --> S2
    P3 --> S3
    P3 -->|"Trigger"| I1
    I1 --> I2
    I1 --> I3
    I3 --> S1
```

**Engineering Reading of the Diagram:**
- The **Gateway** publishes to the **PaaS (AWS IoT Core)** broker.
- The **Rules Engine (PaaS)** fans out data to **SaaS dashboards** for end-users.
- The same PaaS layer can trigger **IaaS-hosted ML models** for deep analytics.
- This **hybrid model** (PaaS + IaaS + SaaS) is the **industry standard** for IoT.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right Cloud Model for an IoT Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Do you need a ready-made app?"}
    Q2{"Do you have a dev team but no sysadmin team?"}
    Q3{"Do you need full control over OS and networking?"}
    R1["CHOOSE SAAS - Ubidots, ThingSpeak, Blynk"]
    R2["CHOOSE PAAS - AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub"]
    R3["CHOOSE IAAS - AWS EC2, Azure VMs, DigitalOcean"]
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3
    Q3 -- No --> R2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with one suitable example for each in the context of IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Service Model | Manages | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)** | Provider manages hardware, network, and storage. User manages OS, middleware, runtime, and applications. | **AWS EC2** — used to host a custom MQTT broker for a factory IoT network. |
| **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)** | Provider manages everything up to the OS. User only develops and deploys the application and manages data. | **AWS IoT Core / Azure IoT Hub** — used to register devices and ingest sensor messages via MQTT. |
| **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)** | Provider manages the entire stack. User only configures and uses the software via a browser. | **Ubidots / ThingSpeak** — ready-to-use dashboard to visualize sensor data. |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
**Explain the "Shared Responsibility Model" in cloud computing. Who is responsible for securing the Operating System in IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Shared Responsibility Model** defines which security and management tasks are handled by the **cloud provider** versus the **cloud consumer**. The division shifts depending on the service model:

- In **IaaS**, the **consumer is responsible** for securing the Operating System, middleware, runtime, and the application — since the user has full control from the OS upward.
- In **PaaS**, the **provider is responsible** for securing the OS, middleware, and runtime. The consumer only secures their application code and data.
- In **SaaS**, the **provider secures the entire stack** (application, OS, runtime, infrastructure). The consumer is responsible only for **user access control (identity, passwords, MFA)** and protecting their own data.

This model ensures clarity of accountability and is essential for audit and compliance in IoT deployments.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered architecture of cloud service models**. Clearly mark the layers managed by the provider and the user in each model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart agriculture company is deploying **50,000 soil-moisture sensors** across multiple farms. The team needs to (i) register and authenticate devices, (ii) store time-series data for 5 years, (iii) run a custom ML model for crop disease prediction, and (iv) provide farmers a mobile-friendly dashboard. **Recommend a suitable cloud service model for each requirement with justification.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Layered Architecture of Cloud Service Models (7 marks):**

The cloud stack can be visualized as **eight layers**, from the physical data center at the bottom to the application interface at the top. The boundary between **provider** and **user** shifts depending on the service model.

**Block Diagram Representation:**

```
+-----------------------------------------+
|  APPLICATION LAYER                      |  ← User (IaaS, PaaS) | Provider (SaaS)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  DATA LAYER                             |  ← User (all models)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  RUNTIME LAYER (JRE, Python, Node)      |  ← User (IaaS) | Provider (PaaS, SaaS)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  MIDDLEWARE (Tomcat, WebSphere)         |  ← User (IaaS) | Provider (PaaS, SaaS)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  OPERATING SYSTEM (Linux, Windows)      |  ← User (IaaS) | Provider (PaaS, SaaS)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  VIRTUALIZATION (Hypervisor)            |  ← Provider (all models)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  SERVERS, STORAGE, NETWORKING           |  ← Provider (all models)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  PHYSICAL DATA CENTER (Power, Cooling)  |  ← Provider (all models)
+-----------------------------------------+
```

**Explanation of Layered Architecture:**

1. **Application Layer:** The end-user software. In IaaS and PaaS, the user builds it; in SaaS, the provider delivers it.
2. **Data Layer:** Always managed by the user, though in SaaS the provider offers tools to manipulate it.
3. **Runtime Layer:** Provides the execution environment (e.g., JRE, Python interpreter). Managed by user in IaaS, provider in PaaS/SaaS.
4. **Middleware Layer:** Software glue like web servers and message brokers. Same split as runtime.
5. **Operating System:** User manages in IaaS; provider manages in PaaS/SaaS.
6. **Virtualization Layer:** Always the provider's domain — the hypervisor (KVM, VMware, Hyper-V) that creates VMs.
7. **Servers/Storage/Networking:** Physical hardware — always the provider's.
8. **Physical Data Center:** Facility, power, cooling, physical security — always the provider's.

The **control-convenience trade-off** is clearly visible: IaaS gives the user maximum control over the top 5 layers, while SaaS leaves the user with only the application/data concern.

---

**(b) Cloud Service Model Recommendation for Smart Agriculture (7 marks):**

| Requirement | Recommended Model | Specific Service | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Register & authenticate 50,000 devices** | **PaaS** | **AWS IoT Core / Azure IoT Hub** | PaaS offers a managed device registry, X.509 certificate-based authentication, and MQTT broker out-of-the-box. Building this on raw IaaS would require a full DevOps team. |
| **(ii) Store 5 years of time-series data** | **IaaS + PaaS hybrid** | **AWS S3 (IaaS storage)** + **AWS Timestream (PaaS DB)** | S3 provides cheap, durable long-term archival (e.g., \$0.023/GB/month). Timestream offers a managed time-series DB for fast queries. Pure SaaS rarely offers 5-year retention economically. |
| **(iii) Custom ML model for crop disease prediction** | **IaaS** | **AWS EC2 (GPU instance) + SageMaker (PaaS ML)** | Custom ML needs full control over frameworks (TensorFlow/PyTorch), GPU drivers, and model versioning. EC2 gives this control; SageMaker accelerates training. |
| **(iv) Mobile-friendly dashboard for farmers** | **SaaS** | **Ubidots / Losant / Power BI embedded** | Farmers are non-technical end-users. SaaS provides ready mobile apps, push notifications, and zero installation. |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** The optimal IoT cloud strategy is a **hybrid mix** — **PaaS for device management**, **IaaS for custom analytics and archival**, and **SaaS for end-user visualization**. This balances cost, control, and convenience across the system's lifecycle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **three cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)** with a real-world analogy and explain how the **shared responsibility model** varies across them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS on the basis of **flexibility, scalability, cost, target audience, vendor lock-in, and IoT suitability**. Present the comparison in a **tabular form with at least 6 parameters**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Three Cloud Service Models — Analogy & Shared Responsibility (7 marks):**

**Real-World Analogy (Pizza-as-a-Service):**

- **On-Premise (no cloud):** You buy ingredients, make dough, and bake pizza at home. You handle **everything**.
- **IaaS:** You rent a **commercial kitchen** with pre-heated ovens and gas. You bring the dough, sauce, and toppings, and cook yourself. The kitchen (infrastructure) is managed by the owner; the recipe (OS, software) is yours.
- **PaaS:** You get a **pre-made pizza base, sauce, and an oven**. You only add toppings and bake. The base/sauce/oven (platform) is managed; toppings (application) are yours.
- **SaaS:** You **order a finished pizza online**. You just eat. Nothing to manage, nothing to cook.

**Shared Responsibility Model Variation:**

| Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application & Data | User | User | User (only data) |
| Runtime, Middleware, OS | **User** | **Provider** | **Provider** |
| Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Network | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Physical Data Center | Provider | Provider | Provider |

In **IaaS**, the user bears the largest operational burden — patching the OS, configuring firewalls, hardening the runtime — making it suitable for teams with strong system administration skills. In **PaaS**, the provider handles OS patching and runtime maintenance; the user focuses only on application code. In **SaaS**, the provider handles the entire stack; the user manages only access control and data. The **security responsibility boundary** clearly shifts upward as the abstraction level rises.

---

**(b) Comparative Analysis — 6 Parameter Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Flexibility** | **Very High** — user can install any OS, middleware, and custom software | **Moderate** — limited to the platform's supported languages and frameworks | **Very Low** — features are fixed by the vendor |
| **2. Scalability** | Manual or scripted (e.g., AWS Auto Scaling Groups) | **Automatic** — built-in horizontal scaling | **Automatic and transparent** — handled by vendor |
| **3. Cost Model** | Pay per VM-hour, GB storage, bandwidth (utility) | Pay per app instance, request, or DB throughput | **Flat subscription** per user/month (e.g., \$15/user) |
| **4. Target Audience** | System admins, network architects, DevOps engineers | Application developers, data engineers | **End users, business teams, non-technical staff** |
| **5. Vendor Lock-in** | **Low** — VMs can be migrated using standard images (OVA, AMI) | **Medium to High** — proprietary APIs, data formats | **Very High** — switching SaaS providers means data migration and retraining |
| **6. IoT Suitability** | Best for **hyperscale, custom protocol, edge analytics** projects (e.g., smart city) | Best for **standard IoT pipelines** (MQTT ingestion, device shadows, rule engine) | Best for **non-technical end-user dashboards** (farmers viewing soil moisture) |
| **7. Example** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine | AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Heroku | Gmail, Salesforce, Ubidots, ThingSpeak |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** No single model is universally superior. The choice depends on the **scale, customization needs, in-house skill set, and budget** of the IoT project. Most production IoT systems use a **hybrid combination** of all three.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (READ CAREFULLY):**
>
> 1. **Conflating PaaS and SaaS:** Many students wrongly write that the user manages the OS in PaaS. **Correct answer:** In PaaS, the **provider** manages the OS. The user only manages **application + data**.
>
> 2. **Giving generic examples:** Writing "IaaS = AWS" is incomplete. Write the **specific service** — e.g., "IaaS = **AWS EC2**" or "IaaS = **Azure Virtual Machines**." Full marks require specificity.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the on-premise baseline:** When comparing models, mention that the **on-premise model** is the baseline (user manages **everything**). This shows conceptual clarity.
>
> 4. **Skipping the responsibility matrix:** A 14-mark question **must** include a clear table or block diagram showing **which layer the user vs. provider manages** in each model. Marks are specifically awarded for this.
>
> 5. **Not linking to IoT:** Since this is an **IoT course (PEAET785)**, every answer **must** include at least **one IoT-specific example or use-case**. Generic cloud answers lose marks.
>
> 6. **Spelling NIST wrong:** It is **"National Institute of Standards and Technology"** (NIST), not "National Institute of Standard Technology."

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **NIST** defines the three service models: **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS** (SP 800-145).
- ✅ **IaaS** = Renting **hardware/virtualization** (e.g., AWS EC2). User manages OS and above.
